Flash will likely fold weaker holdings and may call with AK or AQ, both of which are hands I beat or split with. This is standard value betting strategy in position on the river.

I am currently holding a pair of Jacks (third pair) on a board of A-K-J-8-6. Throughout this hand, I have checked and called Claude's bets on the flop and turn. On the river, Claude has fired a third barrel of 2000 into a pot of 6100. While the pot odds are attractive (I need to be right about 20% of the time), this board texture is extremely favorable for a range that calls a 3-bet pre-flop, containing many Ax, Kx, and even the Broadway straight (QT). Claude's consistent sizing suggests value, and there are very few hands I beat that would realistically triple-barrel here. My pair of Jacks is a weak bluff-catcher in this scenario, so I will fold to preserve my remaining 10,000 chips.

I am on the button with K4o. While a King-high hand has some value in a 3-handed game, K4o is generally too weak to open-raise with aggressive players like Minni and Claude in the blinds. Minni has shown a tendency to play back at raises, and Claude is the chip leader and plays quite loosely. Folding is the most disciplined play here to avoid being caught in a marginal situation out of position or facing a 3-bet.

Heads-up preflop in the small blind with Qc9c — a strong, playable heads-up hand (queen-high, suited, decent connectivity). Against a single big blind I want initiative and to deny cheap equity. Open-raising is standard here to isolate and take the lead postflop. I’ll raise to 300 total (≈3x the BB) — big enough to apply pressure and fold out weaker hands, but small enough to keep the pot controllable against Claude’s deeper stack. Postflop plan: continuation-bet many boards if called; fold to a large 3-bet (or proceed cautiously vs small 3-bets and reevaluate on texture).

Defending BB against aggressive SB raiser. Ac5c has decent equity against Minni's wide range, getting 2:1 odds. Need to avoid being exploited by folding too much from BB. Can play accurately post-flop with position advantage.

I opened preflop and have Qc9c on Jc-2c-5d — a very strong club draw (two clubs on board + two in my hand = 9 outs, ~35% to hit by the river) and initiative as the preflop raiser. This is a dry board where a continuation bet will get value from worse hands (Jx, single overcards, floats) and charge any backdoors. I want to build the pot with my draw while retaining fold equity, so I’ll make a standard ~1/2 pot c-bet. If Claude responds with a large check-raise I’ll re-evaluate (fold to an extreme raise, call smaller raises if the odds justify it).

I'm defending the big blind against Minni's flop c-bet with Ac5c on Jc2c5d. I have a pair of fives with an ace kicker, plus a backdoor flush draw (two clubs). I'm getting 2:1 odds (300 to call into 900 pot), which provides sufficient equity for a call even if my hand is behind to some portion of Minni's range. Minni has been aggressive throughout the session but the board is reasonably coordinated with two clubs. I maintain position advantage post-flop and can play accurately depending on runout. Given stack sizes and pot odds, calling is superior to both folding (exploitable from BB) and raising (my hand isn't strong enough to generate value or build the pot with my marginal holding).

Turn action with Qc9c on Jc-2c-5d-6s: I have a strong four-flush (9 club outs → ~19.6% to hit the river) plus a few pair-up outs, and I have initiative (opened pre and c‑bet the flop). This is a textbook semi-bluff spot: betting wins the pot now vs many marginal hands and builds a pot where I have real equity if called. Pot is 1200 — I’ll make a ~1/2‑pot continuation bet (600). Size is large enough to generate fold equity but small enough to keep the pot controllable given my stack (~8.4k). If Claude check‑raises big (showing a strong made hand) I’ll fold; if he calls I proceed to the river and re-evaluate based on the card and his sizing.

Calling with Ac5c on Jc2c5d6s. I hold bottom pair with an ace kicker and a backdoor flush draw (two clubs). Minni has been aggressive throughout the session, but I'm getting 4:1 pot odds (risking 600 to win 2400 in existing pot). This is sufficient equity breakeven for my hand, which likely has 25-35% equity against her range (mixture of made hands like overpairs, two-pair possibilities if she holds 66, and various semi-bluffs). The turn card (6s) doesn't dramatically help or hurt my position. I maintain positional advantage post-flop on the river and can make accurate decisions based on the final runout. Folding here would be too exploitable given Minni's aggressive tendencies, while raising with this marginal hand would be overcommitting chips without sufficient value or fold equity. The call is optimal given pot odds, hand equity, and stack dynamics.

I have Qc9c and the river paired my 9 (board Jc-2c-5d-6s-9s) giving me second pair with a Queen kicker after missing the club flush. I was the aggressor earlier (c-bet flop, semi-bluff turn) and Claude has merely called both streets — a very passive line that’s consistent with Jx, a medium pocket pair, or cautious overcards. Betting the river would mainly get folds from pure air and calls from hands that already beat me (Jx, two pair, sets) — so it’s thin value and high risk of getting called/raised by better. With only a marginal made hand and strong possibility Claude has a better pair, the lowest-variance, most +EV line is to check back and realize showdown value rather than bloat the pot or turn my hand into a bluff catcher against a competent caller. Action: check to show at showdown and avoid losing more to stronger holdings.

River check with bottom pair and ace kicker. Minni's turn raise followed by river check suggests she's taking this to showdown. Betting accomplishes nothing - hands better than mine will call/raise, hands worse than mine already folded. My pair has showdown value; going to showdown is optimal given her range and action pattern.

I’m on the button with 4♠3♥. Blinds are 50/100 so the pot is 150 and it costs 100 to just call. 4-3o is a weak holding, but in a 3-handed spot with positional advantage it's a good candidate for a standard button steal. A minimum raise to 200 (small amount relative to my 10,175 stack) puts pressure on the blinds, reduces the field, and lets me play postflop last if called. Limping is too passive — this hand plays poorly multiway — and folding is overly tight when I can try to take the blinds. Plan: raise to 200; fold if Claude 3-bets heavily, otherwise proceed cautiously postflop and leverage position.

Folding A6s from small blind against button raise. This is a weak holding played out of position. Despite decent pot odds, the positional disadvantage is significant - I'll have to act first post-flop in a wide range of scenarios. With a healthy 17,750 stack and 110 hands remaining, there's no urgency to play marginal hands. This is a disciplined fold that preserves chips for better spots.

I am in the big blind with 9h4h, which is a suited hand with some playability. Minni has raised to 200 from the button, meaning it's 100 more for me to call into a pot of 350. The pot odds are 3.5:1, which are quite favorable for defending the big blind, especially with a suited hand. While 94 is weak, the low cost of the call and my position in the blind justify a defend to see a flop.

I am in the big blind with 9h4h on a flop of 7d-2c-Jc. I have no pair and no real draw. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Minni), the standard play is to check and see how she reacts. I have no reason to lead out with a bet here given my lack of equity.

I raised preflop as a button steal and was called; on Jc-7d-2c I have 4♠3♥ — no pair and only remote runner-runner equity. Flash checked, so I have meaningful fold equity. This is a textbook spot for a small continuation bet to try to take down the pot against his wide calling range. I’ll make a 1/3-pot c-bet (150 into 450) to pressure unpaired overcards and medium hands while keeping the price low if called. If he folds I win the pot; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and play cautiously (check behind often); if he raises significantly I’ll fold this bluff given my negligible showdown value.

I have 9-high with no pair and no draws (straight or flush) on a 7-2-J board. Minni has bet 150 into a 450 pot. While the price is relatively cheap (4:1 odds), I am out of position with virtually zero equity and no realistic way to improve to the best hand. There is no reason to continue with this hand.

Folding J6o from button. While I have positional advantage, this is a weak off-suit hand that plays poorly postflop even in position. The hand lacks the strength and playability needed to justify getting involved at 50/100 blinds. Better spots will present themselves, and I should preserve chips for premium hands or better situations.

I am in the Small Blind with 5h 2d. This is one of the weakest possible starting hands. Although it only costs 50 more to see a flop, playing such a weak hand out of position against Minni is generally a losing strategy. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with K3s. In a 3-handed game, this is a strong opening hand, but Minni (SB) and Claude (BB) are already in for the blinds. While I could raise to steal, I want to keep the pot manageable or see if I can outplay them post-flop if they are tight. However, usually, a raise on the button is standard to put pressure on the blinds. Looking at the previous hands, Claude and Minni have been active. I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand and potentially win the blinds immediately.

I’m in the small blind with Qs7s facing a button open to 300 (to call 250 into a 450 pot) and the big blind still to act. Q7s is a marginal suited hand that’s frequently dominated by the button’s opening range (lots of Qx, broadways, Ax) and it performs poorly out of position — particularly if Claude (BB) defends and we’re left multiway. Calling requires ~35.7% equity (250 into a 700 pot post-call) and I don’t want to invest out of position with a weak kicker and limited playability. A 3-bet bluff is also unattractive: Q7s has poor blocker value and BB looks likely to defend, so I’d be building a big pot with a subpar hand. Preserve stack for better spots and fold here.

Calling with pocket nines in the big blind against a button raise. This hand plays well in position post-flop, and I have excellent pot odds (2.25:1). By calling rather than 3-betting, I can play positional poker on the flop where I have a skill advantage, while keeping the pot size manageable. The hand strength justifies seeing a flop, and my ability to control the action with position outweighs the benefit of isolating preflop.

I'm holding pocket nines in the BB against Flash's button raise. The flop is 5c 2c Ks - a dry board with an overcard that could connect with Flash's preflop raising range. Importantly, Flash has position on me post-flop. With a vulnerable hand (pocket pair), I should check here to keep the pot manageable and gather information about Flash's hand. If Flash has a king or missed completely, their action will tell me a lot. If they bet, I have position information and pot odds to make a decision. If they check, we proceed to the turn with a smaller pot. Checking is the prudent play when out of position against a positional opponent with a hand that's good but not great.

I have top pair (Kings) on a board of 5c-2c-Ks. While my kicker is weak (3d), in a three-handed game, top pair is a very strong hand. Claude checked to me after calling my pre-flop raise. I should bet here to get value from weaker hands (like a King with a worse kicker, or smaller pairs) and to protect against the club flush draw. A bet of around 60% of the pot is appropriate to build the pot while keeping it somewhat controlled.

Calling the 400-chip bet on the flop with pocket nines against Flash's aggression. The board is dry (5c 2c Ks), limiting draws and runaway situations. I'm getting 3.625:1 odds and need approximately 28% equity - pocket nines likely exceeds this against Flash's wide range that includes both value hands (K-x, broadway) and bluffs. Calling keeps the pot manageable while allowing me to see the turn card and reassess. This is superior to folding a reasonable hand at good odds, and better than raising with a vulnerable hand that could easily be crushed by Flash's stronger holdings.

Checking on the turn with pocket nines. The Ace is a bad card for my holding - it likely improved Flash's pre-flop raising range (AK, AQ, broadway pairs). Being out of position against an aggressive opponent with a vulnerable middle pair, checking is the prudent play. This allows me to keep the pot manageable, gather information about Flash's hand strength through their river action, and avoid overcommitting with marginal equity. If Flash has it, they'll bet the river and I can make a fold decision. If they check through, I get a cheap showdown.

The Ace on the turn is a scare card that likely hits Claude's range more than mine, as he called pre-flop and on the flop. Since I have top pair (now second pair) with a weak kicker, I have significant showdown value but little incentive to build a larger pot. By checking back, I control the pot size and can potentially see a cheap showdown or reassess on the river if Claude bets.

Checking down to showdown with pocket 9s. Flash's turn check indicates weakness or a hand they want to show down. My hand is decent but not strong enough to build value on a river bet. The board (5c 2c Ks Ah 7c) is coordinated with Flash's preflop raising range, and they've shown they're either: (1) a medium-strength hand wanting to get to showdown, (2) a complete miss, or (3) slowplaying (unlikely given the turn check). Betting here either gets folded by hands that would call anyway or gets called by better holdings. Checking minimizes loss if I'm behind and goes to showdown at the most appropriate equity point.

The river completes the club flush draw (7c) on a board that already contains an Ace (Ah) from the turn. My hand, Kd 3d, was top pair on the flop but is now at best second pair with a weak kicker. Claude has checked to me twice after calling my flop bet. While his checks could indicate weakness, there is very little value in betting here; any hand that calls me (an Ace, a better King, or a flush) has me beaten, and hands that I beat (lower pairs or missed draws) are likely to fold. Checking back allows me to realize my showdown value and minimizes risk on a coordinated board.

We’re on the button in a 3-handed pot with K♠9♣ — this is a hand I want to play in position and it’s strong enough to open. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x BB): that size takes the initiative, applies pressure to the blinds and helps isolate the big blind, while keeping the pot manageable relative to our deep stack. If I get called I’ll C‑bet favorable flops and continue when I hit a king or pick up equity; if I face a sizable 3‑bet I’ll fold rather than bloat the pot with a marginal kicker. Overall this is an exploitative, position-first play aiming for long‑term EV across the session.

4-6 offsuit is one of the worst hands in poker. While I'm only risking 250 more chips to call from the small blind, this hand has poor equity against Minni's raising range and I'll be out of position for the entire hand. Minni has been active and aggressive throughout the session, and I have a strong chip position at 17,000. This is a clear fold - I should only call or raise with hands that have real equity. Preserving chips with disciplined hand selection is crucial for long-term profit, especially when I can expect better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with K4o. Minni has raised to 3BB from the button. While K4o is not a premium hand, it has some value defending against a button open, especially with the pot odds offered (200 to call a 450 pot). However, against a 3x open, K4o is often a fold or a low-frequency 3-bet bluff. Looking at Minni's previous hands, she is quite active. But given our position and the weakness of K4o, I will choose to fold and wait for a better spot to avoid playing a bloated pot out of position.

I'm on the button with K6o (king-six offsuit), facing a 100 chip call into a 150 chip pot. While I have excellent position and decent pot odds, K6o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position postflop in most scenarios. 

Looking at my stack (17000), I have ample chips to be selective. My previous hands show I've been winning through aggressive, positional play with strong hands. K6o lacks the strength and playability to justify entering the pot. Even with position, I'd likely be out of position on the flop if both blinds call, and the hand has poor equity against most opponent ranges.

With 106 hands remaining and a healthy chip stack, I should maintain high hand standards and avoid marginal situations. This is a clear fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 2d 7s. Claude has already folded from the Button. This leaves only me and Minni (the Big Blind) in the hand. 2-7 offsuit is the statistically worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. While I only need to call 50 more chips to see a flop, this hand has extremely poor playability and rarely wins at showdown. Given the weak nature of the hand, it is standard and correct to fold rather than investing more chips out of position.

I am on the button with 6d 5s, a speculative hand. The table has been relatively aggressive pre-flop, but my position allows me some leverage. However, 65o is quite weak for a standard open or a call against blinds that have shown a willingness to 3-bet or play large pots (especially Claude). Given the stack depths and the fact that I want to minimize variance with marginal holdings while waiting for better spots, folding is the most disciplined play here.

I’m in the small blind with 2♣6♣ heads-up vs Claude (BB). This is a weak but suited hand; heads-up we should defend very widely, and the cost to see a flop is only 50 into a 150 pot. Raising to 200 is an option to seize initiative, but Claude has shown willingness to reraise/call big — I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with a marginal holding. I’ll call the extra 50 (100 total committed) and see a flop. Plan: continue only with clear equity — a club/paired board or a disguised two-pair/set/draw — and fold to heavy aggression on dry, unfavorable runouts to protect the stack.

With pocket 3s in heads-up play against Minni from the big blind, I check to see the flop cheaply. Small pairs are vulnerable postflop and benefit from seeing the board economically. This lets me evaluate my hand strength based on the community cards without committing additional chips preflop with a marginal holding.

I defended preflop with 2♣6♣ heads-up to see a cheap flop, but this board (9♥ 8♥ 5♥) is very unfavorable for my holding. I have no hearts and no pair — only a 4‑out gutshot to a 7 (≈16% to hit by the river) and only runner‑runner club backdoors. Out of position against Claude and with such limited equity, I shouldn’t bloat the pot. I’ll check and plan to fold to any meaningful bet; if he checks back I’ll reassess on the turn and only continue with clear improvements or correct pot odds for a call.

I'm holding pocket 3s on a 9h 8h 5h flop - an extremely vulnerable hand on a draw-heavy board. With three hearts on the board, any opponent with two hearts has significant equity or even a draw to beat me. Minni checked the flop, indicating weakness, which suggests this isn't a great moment to apply pressure with my marginal holding. Betting would be thin value at best and risks getting called by superior hands. By checking, I can control the pot size with this weak pair, see another card cheaply, and reassess on the turn with more information. This line is cost-effective and reduces variance with a hand that's likely either ahead of Minni's absolute trash or behind her draws.

Check. I defended preflop with 2♣6♣ to see a cheap flop and have followed my plan: I have nothing on 9♥‑8♥‑5♥‑Q♠ — no pair, no hearts, and only four raw outs (the sevens) to make a 5‑6‑7‑8‑9 straight on the river (~8–9% equity). Note that one of those outs is 7♥ and could still be dominated if Claude holds hearts (the flop already has three hearts), so my real equity may be even lower. Claude’s flop/turn checks show passivity but could be slow play; this is a very dangerous, heart‑heavy board to bluff into. Betting now would be a thin bluff into a 200 pot with very little fold equity — a min bet of 100 requires roughly a 33% fold frequency to be profitable versus no equity, while my chance to improve is only ~9%, so calling a bet would be wildly incorrect. I’ll check and plan to fold to any meaningful bet; only continue if I hit the river or if I get a very small and exploitable action from Claude.

Pocket 3s is an extremely weak hand on this draw-heavy board (9h 8h 5h Qs). With three hearts on the flop, any opponent holding two hearts has significant equity or a flush draw. Minni has demonstrated weakness by checking both the flop and turn, suggesting she either holds marginal holdings or is on a draw herself. Betting here would be thin value at best—I'm unlikely to fold the vast majority of hands that call, and if she does call, I'm rarely ahead. Checking allows me to see the river at no additional cost and keep the pot manageable with a hand that's vulnerable to many turn/river cards. This line minimizes variance and losses with a weak pair while maintaining the option to check down or re-evaluate based on river action.

I paired my deuce on the river (2♣ in hand with 2♠ on board) — bottom pair on 9♥‑8♥‑5♥‑Q♠‑2♠. Claude checked flop and turn, which signals weakness or pot‑control rather than a proactive value range (he tends to bet/raise big with strong hands). Bottom pair has modest showdown value heads‑up and will beat many missed heart draws and air, plus some weaker one‑pair hands (9x/8x/5x) that checked as well. A small value bet is the lowest‑risk way to extract from those hands while keeping losses limited if I’m up against Qx or a bigger pair. I’ll bet half the pot (100) to get value; if Claude shows aggression (a raise) I’ll fold this weak pair.

Facing a river raise to 100 with pocket threes on a final board of 9h 8h 5h Qs 2s. Minni's action line is telling: she called preflop, then checked both the flop on a draw-heavy three-heart board and the turn after the Qs came. Her river raise of only 100 is a small bet relative to the 300 pot, giving me immediate odds of 3:1. This small bet size suggests either a weak hand trying to steal a small pot or a bluff rather than a strong made hand. While my pair of threes is vulnerable, calling 100 to win 300 requires only ~25% equity. Given Minni's passive line and the minimal bet size, she likely either has a weak pair herself, missed a draw, or is semi-bluffing. My threes have showdown value here. The pot odds and her betting pattern make this a profitable call rather than folding the weak pair outright.

Button with Ts8s — a suited 10-x hand with good postflop playability and position against only the blinds. Folding is too nitty; limping surrenders initiative and invites multiway action where this hand loses equity. I open to 300 (≈3x the BB) to apply pressure, steal the blinds, and play in position. Plan: c-bet favorable flops and use position to realize equity; fold to a large, polarized 3‑bet (I’m out of position and dominated), but call small-to-medium 3‑bets occasionally depending on sizing and opponent tendencies. 300 keeps the raise standard-sized for the table and preserves maneuverability with deep stacks.

I'm in the small blind with A8o (Ace-Eight offsuit) against Minni's button raise to 300. 

Key factors:
1. **Hand strength**: A8o is a decent playable hand with strong ace high potential
2. **Position**: Small blind is early position, but I'll have a positional advantage post-flop against Minni
3. **Pot odds**: I need to call 250 to win 450 (1.8:1 odds), requiring ~35% equity. Against Minni's wide button range, I should have 40-45% equity
4. **Minni's tendencies**: Throughout the hands, Minni has been aggressive from late position with a wide range
5. **Stack situation**: No short stacks, good room to play

**Action plan**: Call the raise. This allows me to:
- See a flop with reasonable equity
- Maintain flexibility in a multiway pot (Flash still to act in big blind)
- Use positional advantage post-flop
- Keep variance manageable on a favorable pot odds situation

Raising would be aggressive but risks inflating the pot out of position. Folding A8o would be too tight given the favorable odds and equity.

I am in the Big Blind with a pocket pair of Sevens (7s 7c). Minni (Button) raised to 300, and Claude (Small Blind) called. The pot is currently 700, and it costs me 200 more to call. I have a medium pocket pair in a multi-way pot. While I could raise for value and protection, calling is a very solid option to see a flop cheaply with a 'set-mining' strategy and to keep the pot manageable. Since I'm out of position against Minni, I'll elect to call and evaluate based on the flop texture.

On the flop 4c-2h-4s with A8o, I'm in a tough spot. The board is dry and doesn't significantly help my hand. I'm holding Ace high, which has some showdown value but is vulnerable to many holdings in this multiway pot (4s on board means trips are possible, and Minni's button range is wide). As first to act out of position in a 3-way pot, checking is the correct play because: (1) I want to control the pot size with a marginal hand, (2) I can gather information before committing more chips, (3) checking allows Minni and Flash to continue with their weaker holdings if they wish, and (4) if I'm beat, I minimize losses. This play keeps me flexible for subsequent streets while respecting that my hand isn't strong enough to lead into two opponents.

I have an overpair to the board (7s 7c) on a paired board (4c 2h 4s). This is a strong hand in a multi-way pot. Claude checked from the Small Blind. I am the Big Blind. Minni, the pre-flop aggressor on the button, is next to act. If I bet now, I might force out worse hands or get raised by a 4. Checking allows Minni to continue her aggression with a wide range of hands or allows me to see how she reacts to the board. Given that I have a solid overpair and want to control the pot size while potentially inducing a bluff or a bet from a weaker pair (like pocket 3s, 5s, 6s) or high cards (Ace-high), I will check and see what the button does.

I opened preflop and both blinds called, then both checked the flop. The board (4c 2h 4s) is dry and low — a great spot to continuation-bet as the preflop aggressor. I have two overcards (T8) with some showdown value and a weak backdoor spade possibility, but no pair; my goal is to take down the pot now or charge weaker holdings (A-high, broadways, weak draws) to realize equity. Against two callers I’ll use a small-to-medium c-bet to apply pressure while keeping the pot controllable and leaving room to fold to strong resistance. Betting 300 (~1/3 pot) achieves that: it folds many marginal hands, gets value from worse, and keeps my options open on the turn. If I face a large raise I’ll give it up; if called I’ll re-evaluate on turn and barrel only when I pick up equity or additional fold equity.

I'm calling Minni's flop bet of 300 into a 1200 pot on the 4c-2h-4s board with Ad8c.

Key considerations:
1. **Pot odds**: I'm getting 4:1 (calling 300 to win 1500), needing only ~20% equity to break even. A8 has significantly more equity than that.

2. **Hand assessment**: While A8 is just ace high on this dry, paired flop, it has real showdown value. Against Minni's wide button range, I should win the pot roughly 35-40% of the time, well above the 20% breakeven threshold.

3. **Minni's bet pattern**: Her 300 bet into 1200 is a small, non-committal size. This suggests either a weak holding or a slow-play. Either way, it doesn't scream extreme strength.

4. **Flash's action**: Flash checked, indicating weakness or a medium hand. This reduces the likelihood I'm facing a monster.

5. **Position and implied odds**: While I'm out of position, the deep stacks and weak flop texture provide good implied odds if I hit an ace or 8 on the turn.

6. **Stack efficiency**: With 16900 in my stack and only 300 to call, folding A8 here would be unnecessarily nitty and leaves chips on the table in a favorable spot.

Raising would overrepresent my hand strength; folding surrenders too much value. Calling allows me to gather more information and potentially improve while maintaining a profitable situation.

I have an overpair (7s 7c) on a paired, low-card board (4c 2h 4s). This is a strong hand in this situation. Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, has made a small continuation bet of 300 into a 900 pot, which is very common. Claude's call suggests he may have a small pocket pair or overcards. By calling, I close the action on the flop, keep the pot size manageable, and allow Minni to continue bluffing or betting with weaker hands on the turn while also protecting against being blown off the hand by a reraise.

On the turn with Ad8c on a 4c-2h-4s-Qs board, I have Ace high with marginal showdown value. The Queen is a neutral card that doesn't significantly improve my hand. With three players remaining in a multiway pot, I should check for several reasons: (1) My hand doesn't warrant a bet - Ace high is vulnerable and won't generate folds from weaker holdings or extract value from worse hands, (2) Pot control is important here - I'm out of position with a medium-strength hand on a relatively wet board, (3) Checking gathers crucial information about opponent holdings before committing more chips to the river, (4) If the turn checks around, I get to see the river for free with decent equity, (5) Betting would overrepresent my hand strength and invite difficult decisions. Checking maintains flexibility: if Minni or Flash bet, I can evaluate the new information; if it checks through, I see showdown value. With deep stacks and a marginal hand, pot control is superior to aggression here.

The turn brings a Queen, which is an overcard to my pocket Sevens. This card likely improves some of the ranges both Claude and Minni could be holding, such as AQ, KQ, or QJ. I have some showdown value with my pair, but the board is now more dangerous. Claude has checked to me. I will check as well to control the pot size and see what Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, decides to do. If she checks behind, I can evaluate the river. If she bets, I'll have to consider the bet size and the likelihood that she or Claude has improved to a Queen or holds a 4.

I opened preflop and c‑bet the dry 4c‑2h‑4s flop; both callers checked the turn. The Qs gives me a very strong draw: I hold Ts8s so I have nine spade outs to make a flush on the river (≈9/46 ≈ 19.6%) plus 3 tens and 3 eights as extra pair outs (≈15/46 ≈ 32.6% raw improvement chance). Against two checked opponents I have reasonable fold equity and I’ve picked up meaningful equity, so I should continue as a semi‑bluff rather than check and give up initiative. I’ll make a modest, posture‑balancing bet (~1/3 pot) to fold out weak A‑highs and small pairs while keeping the pot controllable if called by one or both. If I face a large raise I’ll give up the draw; if I get called I’ll reevaluate on the river for value/bluff depending on the card.

Calling Minni's 600 turn bet with Ad8c on 4c-2h-4s-Qs. I'm getting 4:1 pot odds, needing only 20% equity to break even, and I likely have 32-35% equity against Minni's range. Ace high has reasonable showdown value, and I have 6 outs for improvement (any Ace or 8). Folding A8 here surrenders significant value in a favorable spot. Flash's weakness (checked twice) suggests I'm not in a hopeless situation. With deep stacks remaining, calling allows me to gather more information on the river and potentially improve. This is standard +EV poker.

The turn Queen is an overcard to my pocket Sevens, which weakens my hand significantly. However, Minni's bet of 600 into a 2400 pot (after Claude's call) offers me excellent pot odds of 5:1. Claude's call suggests he has a piece of the board, likely a small pair or a Queen, but the price is cheap enough to see a river card and check for a possible showdown if the final card is favorable and the action remains passive.

River with Ad8c on 4c-2h-4s-Qs-Kc. I have Ace-King, a marginal showdown hand. Both opponents checked the turn, suggesting neither has a premium holding, though the river King could have assisted them. Betting with this weak hand accomplishes nothing—no worse hands fold, and I gain no value from better holdings. Checking to showdown is the correct play, allowing me to see if my Ace-King wins without losing additional chips. This is a pot control spot where aggression is counterproductive with a hand that's primarily value-based on showdown equity.

I have pocket Sevens on a board that now contains two overcards (Queen and King) and is paired with Fours. I have played the hand passively so far, check-calling the flop and turn bets from Minni. Claude, acting first on the river, has checked. Given that the board texture has significantly improved many of the overcards that Minni (the pre-flop aggressor) would likely be betting (such as KQ, QJ, or AK), my pocket Sevens have very little value as a bet and are now a weak bluff-catcher at best. I will check to see Minni's action. If she checks behind, I may win against some missed draws or smaller pairs; if she bets, I will likely fold as I am sandwiched between her and Claude.

I hold Ts8s and missed my draw. The turn Qs gave me a 4-card spade draw but the river Kc bricks — I have no pair (T-high) in a three-way pot of 3,600. Both opponents called my flop and turn bets and then checked the river, which usually indicates at least some showdown value (pairs/Qx/etc.) and a willingness to call river pressure. In a multiway pot a bluff needs both players to fold to be profitable; given their passive calls on earlier streets my fold equity is low. I preserve chips and the option of winning at showdown by checking; if faced with a meaningful bet I’ll fold this missed-draw hand.

Pocket aces is the strongest possible starting hand. I'm in position on the button with a healthy 16,000 chip stack facing blinds of 14,725 and 9,125. A standard raise to 4x the big blind (400 chips) is the correct play here - it builds the pot while I have maximum equity, establishes aggression, and gives me the best expected value. This is straightforward value poker with the nuts pre-flop.

In a 3-handed game, King-Three suited is a reasonable hand to defend from the Small Blind against a Button open, especially with the potential for a flush and high-card value. Although I'll be out of position, the pot odds (350 to call into a 550 pot) make it worth seeing a flop.

Qc5c is a weak, marginal hand to defend out of the big blind in a 3‑way pot against a button open and a small‑blind cold call. I’m out of position to both opponents and my hand is frequently dominated (Qx, Ax, Kx) with poor multiway equity and reverse implied odds. It costs 300 to call into a 900 pot (≈3:1), but post‑flop playability is so poor here that the pot odds don’t justify continuing. A light squeeze to 700 would be ineffective — too small to fold out two callers and undesirable OOP with Q5s. I’ll fold and preserve my ~91BB stack for better spots.

On a flop of 6h-Ad-Kd, I have middle pair (Kings) with a low kicker (3). Claude was the pre-flop aggressor from the button, and his range includes many Aces and better Kings. However, I have enough hand strength to see a turn. I will check to him and see how he responds to the board texture. If he bets, I plan to call a reasonably sized bet to see if the turn improves my hand or if he checks back on a later street.

I have flopped trip aces (Ac As with Ad on board) - an extremely strong hand. Flash checked from the small blind, indicating weakness or a cautious approach. Given that Flash called my pre-flop raise and the flop contains an Ace and King, my hand is very well-positioned.

My objective is to extract value from Flash's range, which likely includes medium pairs, broadway cards, or draws. A direct check would allow Flash to see the turn for free - a missed opportunity. I should make a value bet that looks natural and keeps Flash engaged.

I'll bet approximately 2/3 of the current pot (900 chips) - around 600 chips. This bet size:
1. Extracts solid value from Flash's likely holding range
2. Builds the pot while I have the best hand
3. Appears consistent with my pre-flop aggression
4. Isn't so large that it scares Flash away entirely

With 15,600 chips, I can comfortably afford this bet and still maintain a strong stack regardless of outcome.

I have middle pair (Kings) on a board of Ace-King-Six. Claude, as the pre-flop aggressor from the button, is likely to continuation-bet a wide range here, including many bluffs or weaker pairs. Although the presence of the Ace is concerning, my hand is too strong to fold to a single bet in a 3-handed game. I am getting roughly 2.5:1 on a call (600 to win 1500), so I will call to see the turn and re-evaluate based on the next card and Claude's action.

I have middle pair (Kings) with a weak kicker on a board containing an Ace. Since I am out of position against Claude, who was the pre-flop aggressor and bet significantly on the flop, the most prudent course of action is to check and see how he reacts to the turn card. Betting myself would likely only be called by hands that beat me (like Aces or Kings with better kickers), so I will check to realize my equity and potentially reach showdown cheaply.

I have flopped trip aces (Ac As with Ad on board), which is an extremely strong hand. Flash has now checked on both the flop and turn, indicating weakness or a cautious approach with his holding.

The turn brought a 9s, which is unlikely to have improved Flash's hand significantly if he was drawing. Flash's check-check pattern strongly suggests he's in a weak position - possibly a medium pair, a gutshot draw, or complete air.

My previous bet on the flop was 600 and he called. Now with a blank turn card (9s doesn't complete any obvious draws), I should continue to extract value while maintaining a line that keeps Flash engaged.

A bet of 1,000 (approximately 50% of the current 2,100 pot) is appropriate because it:
1. Extracts significant value from a weaker range
2. Isn't so large that it causes Flash to fold all his marginal holdings
3. Builds the pot for a river bet if he calls
4. Represents consistent aggression with the strength of my hand
5. Leaves me with 14,000 chips, plenty of cushion regardless of outcome

I'm confident in betting my nut hand for value here.

On a board of Ace-King-Six-Nine, my middle pair of Kings with a Trey kicker is extremely vulnerable. Claude was the pre-flop aggressor from the button and has shown consistent strength by betting both the flop and the turn. In a 3-handed game, while ranges are wider, this specific board texture hits a button-opening range very hard, containing many superior Aces and Kings. Even if Claude is betting for thin value with a King, his kicker will almost certainly beat my Trey. Given the board contains both an Ace and a King, and Claude is continuing his aggression, the most likely outcome is that I am significantly behind. I will fold now to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with 7h 4h, which is a very weak starting hand. In a 3-player game, although I have positional advantage, 7-4 offsuit is not worth an open-raise or a call against the blinds. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

We're heads-up vs the big blind and I'll have position postflop (I act first pre-flop but last on later streets). 9♠6♠ is a playable, suited hand heads-up with decent postflop playability and flush/straight potential. Folding is too nitty and calling 50 is passive — giving Claude a free or cheap flop. I want the initiative and fold equity, and with deep-ish stacks I can maneuver postflop. A standard 2xBB open to the table min-raise (200) is the right play here: builds the pot, applies pressure, and keeps my range wide in position.

3-7 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker. While the pot offers 3:1 odds, my hand has insufficient equity (~30-35%) to justify a call. With a chip lead and no positional advantage that compensates for hand weakness, folding is the mathematically sound decision to protect my edge in this game.

Three-handed on the button I should be opening a fairly wide range. J♣8♥ is marginal but a perfectly acceptable steal/position hand — it has a live high card, can pick up blinds, and plays well in position. Calling 100 is too passive and folding is too weak; I want to seize initiative and apply pressure to the blinds. I’ll raise to 300 (3x the BB) — a size consistent with the table that gives good fold equity, keeps smaller postflop pots if called, and allows me to c-bet many flops. If I face a large 3-bet from Claude (SB) I’ll fold; I can consider calling a small 3-bet to see a flop because of position and stack depth.

Calling 250 to match Minni's raise. K8o is marginal, but the pot odds (250 to win 450+) are acceptable. I'll have position post-flop if Flash folds, allowing me to control the action. The king has showdown value and improves to a strong hand if it connects. This is a marginal spot that becomes profitable with position and the ability to fold to aggression post-flop if needed.

I am in the Big Blind with 9c 5c. Minni raised from the button to 300 and Claude called from the Small Blind. The pot is currently 700 and it costs me 200 to call. These are excellent pot odds (4.5 to 1), and while 95s is a marginal hand, its suitedness gives it enough playability to justify a defense in a three-way pot, even being out of position.

On a 4h 2h 5s flop with K8o, I have only king-high with no pair or draw. This is a weak hand on a connected, low board that favors calling ranges. I'm out of position (small blind) and have already committed 250 to the pot pre-flop. The best play is to check here and gather information about Minni's and Flash's holdings. If either opponent bets, I can make a more informed decision about whether to call, fold, or raise based on their action patterns and board texture. Betting would be speculative and likely to face aggression. Checking allows me to hit a king on subsequent streets while preserving chips with a marginal holding.

I have bottom pair (5s) with a 9 kicker on a low, connected board (4h 2h 5s) in a three-way pot. My hand has some showdown value but is quite vulnerable. Since I am out of position and the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) is still to act, checking is the most prudent play. This allows me to see how the button reacts to the flop and gauge the strength of my opponents' hands without inflating the pot with a marginal holding.

I opened wide on the button with Jc8h and both blinds just called. Flop is 4h-2h-5s: dry board but two hearts present. I have no pair and only a backdoor heart (very weak improvement chances), so my hand has little showdown value. As the preflop aggressor I should take the initiative and c-bet to fold out pure air and weaker overcards, but because this is a three-way pot and the board contains heart potential I’ll use a small sizing to preserve fold equity while keeping the pot controllable. A ~1/3 pot stab (300 into 900) is ideal: it applies pressure, keeps my range honest, and allows me to fold to strong resistance. Plan: if I get called I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn—continue only if I improve or get a favorable turn; fold to a sizable flop raise since I have no pair and little equity.

K8o on a 4h 2h 5s flop is king-high with no draws. Out of position versus aggressive Minni, I have insufficient equity to justify continuing. The hand has only ~15% equity, below the ~20% required by 4:1 pot odds, and I'm vulnerable to further aggression on later streets. Flash's flop check doesn't guarantee weakness. The pre-flop 250 is sunk cost. Folding preserves my 16,700 stack for premium situations where I have position or hand strength advantage.

I have top pair (fives) with a nine kicker on a low, connected board. Minni's 1/3 pot bet is a standard continuation bet on this texture. With 4-to-1 pot odds, I have a clear call to see how the turn develops. Raising would likely only get called by better hands or strong draws, while calling allows me to keep the pot manageable and potentially realize my showdown value.

On the turn, the board pairs with the Four of Spades. I currently hold two pair (Fives and Fours) with a Nine kicker. While the Four is a card that could have potentially improved my range as the Big Blind defender, I do not actually hold a Four. Checking is the most appropriate action here to see how Minni reacts. If Minni checks back, I can feel better about my showdown value. If she bets, I will have to evaluate the size and her likely range, which includes many overcards that missed this board but also potential trips or overpairs.

I hold 8♥ J♣ and after my 300 c-bet on the flop I was called. Turn is 4♠, pairing the board (4♥-2♥-5♠-4♠). My hand has no pair and the backdoor heart draw is dead – I only have 6 outs (three J’s and three 8’s) ≈ ~13% to improve on the river. Flash’s check on the turn can be weakness or a slow-played made hand (pocket 4/5/2 or a pair), and a second barrel here faces the risk of a raise that I should fold to. Given my earlier plan to proceed cautiously when called and the low equity, I prefer pot control: check and evaluate the river. Plan: fold to meaningful aggression on the turn; if checked through I’ll re-evaluate the river and consider a small bluff only on a very favorable scare card, otherwise check-fold to pressure.

I have two pair (fives and fours) with a nine kicker. The board is quite coordinated, but Minni's check on the turn suggests she likely doesn't have a monster like trips or a high overpair. The Queen on the river is an overcard that might have improved some of her broadway air, but it's more likely she has a mid-pair or a missed draw. I have decent showdown value but little to gain from betting, as better hands will call and worse hands will fold. I will check and hope to take it down at showdown or evaluate a small bet from her.

Board: 4h 2h 5s 4s Qc. I have Jc8h — missed my hand, no pair, and the backdoor heart draw died on the turn. I c-bet the flop and got called; both of us checked the turn. Given that line, Flash’s calling range on the flop likely includes lots of small/medium pairs (2s, 5s, pocket pairs) and some floats. The Q on the river is a somewhat scary card for my perceived range but it actually doesn’t give me any blockers (I don’t hold a Q or a 4/5), so my bluff credibility is weak. Flash has shown tendencies to call down and to defend passively; a bluff here would need significant fold equity to be +EV, and I don’t see convincing evidence of that. Pot control and chip preservation are best: check and fold to meaningful raise (they checked, so I’ll check through).

Pocket queens on the button is one of the strongest starting hands in poker. I'm in excellent position with only Flash (small blind) and Minni (big blind) remaining. The pot is 150 with 100 to call. 

I should raise here to:
1. Build the pot with a premium hand
2. Win the blinds immediately or narrow the field
3. Apply pressure from position
4. Define my hand strength early

A 3x raise to 300 is standard and appropriate given my hand strength and position. This gives good pot odds to opponents if they want to call, but also gives me the option to continue aggressively post-flop with position advantage.

I am in the Small Blind with 2s 7s. Claude has raised from the Button to 300. 2-7 suited is a very weak hand, and playing it out of position against a Button raise is generally a losing strategy. I should fold and wait for a better spot.

Claude (button) opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with 8♦4♦ — suited but very low and poorly connected. Pot is 450 and I’d have to call 200 more (300 total). With ~85BB stacks and acting OOP postflop, this hand is hard to play: it’s dominated by a large portion of a BTN opening range, has mediocre equity vs realistic ranges, and offers poor realization OOP. A tiny 3-bet to the min (500) is a weak bluff that won’t accomplish much and leaves me in a tough spot if called. Given the marginal pot odds (~31% needed) and the postflop disadvantages, I’ll conserve chips and fold this marginal spot.
I am on the button with Ace-Six suited in a 3-handed game. This is a strong starting hand and a standard opening range. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and put pressure on the blinds.

I’m in the small blind with 6♦5♠ facing a 3BB open to 300 from the button, BB still to act. 6-5 offsuit is a marginal, playable hand but I’m out of position — I don’t want to inflate the pot by 3-betting with such a hand because Claude (BB) is sticky/aggressive and could 3-bet or call, putting me in tough spots OOP. Folding surrenders only my 50¢ SB but is overly tight given the cheap price to see a flop. Calling 250 more (300 total) keeps the pot reasonable, preserves stack depth (I have ~85BB), and lets me see a flop where I can continue only if I connect (pair, two pair, straight/strong draw); otherwise I’ll exercise pot control and fold to heavy aggression. So I’ll call to 300 total.

3-betting AQ from big blind. This premium hand should be played aggressively to build the pot and gain control. Flash's aggressive button raise and Minni's call from SB suggest moderate hands. A raise to 750 (600 more chips) is appropriate sizing to get value or take the pot down preflop. This also potentially wins immediately or isolates me against one opponent in position.

I have Ace-Six suited on the button. I opened to 300, and Claude in the big blind put in a small 3-bet to 750. Minni, in the small blind, has already called my initial raise. The price to call is 450 chips into a pot that will be at least 1,800. This is a very favorable price (25% pot odds) for a hand that plays well post-flop and has the potential to make nut flushes. Additionally, I will have position throughout the rest of the hand. I will call to see the flop.

I’m in the small blind with 6♦5♠ facing a 3-bet to 750 from Claude (BB) after Flash opened and then called the 3-bet. I’ve already invested 300, but calling another 450 to play multiway out of position would require ~20% equity (450 into a 2250 pot) and 6-5o has poor realized equity against a 3-bet range and a caller. Claude has been sticky/aggressive historically, and the caller behind reduces fold equity and increases likelihood I’m dominated. A 4-bet bluff to 1200 here is too spewy with this hand and risk profile. Fold and conserve my stack for better spots.

Betting top pair with AQ on an unpaired, relatively dry flop after 3-betting preflop. This hand is strong against Flash's likely calling range (mid-pairs, weaker Aces, Broadway cards). A bet of 1100 (roughly 60% pot) is standard for building value while maintaining pot control from out of position. This also gives me information for future streets while gathering chips from hands I beat.

I have top pair with an Ace on a flop of 4h-Kd-Ad. Pre-flop, I called a 3-bet from Claude, who is now leading out for 1100 into a 1800 pot. While my kicker is weak (6c), folding top pair on the flop in a 3-bet pot while in position is generally too tight. Claude's range here includes strong Aces (AK, AQ, AJ), but could also include semi-bluffs or middle pairs that are testing the water. I will call to see the turn and re-evaluate Claude's aggression.

Betting 2400 on the turn with top pair (pair of Aces) after Flash checked. Flash's check on the turn King suggests weakness—if they had two pair or better, they would have bet or shown strength. My Ah-Qs is strong against Flash's likely range of mid-pairs, weaker Aces, or draws. This bet extracts value from dominated hands, applies pressure, and potentially wins the pot if Flash's check indicated they're surrendering. The sizing of 2400 is approximately 60% of the current pot, which is standard for continuation betting from a strong hand in position advantage. This bet also leaves me room to evaluate the river based on Flash's response.

I have Ace-Six suited and the board is Ad-Kd-4h-Kc. While I have top pair (Aces and Kings), my kicker is very weak. Claude 3-bet from the big blind pre-flop and has lead out on both the flop and the turn with significant sizing. His range for 3-betting and double-barreling on an Ace-King high board heavily favors strong Aces (AQ, AJ, AT) or Kings (AK, KQ). Given that most of his value range dominates my 6-kicker and there are few natural bluffs he would play this way, especially after I called the flop, I am likely behind. Calling 2400 chips here would commit a large portion of my stack with very little equity or hope of a split. I will fold.

Button with 9♦7♣ in a 3-handed game is a playable, positionally advantaged hand. With only the blinds to beat and stacks deep, I want to apply pressure and steal the blinds while avoiding giving opponents cheap odds or encouraging multiway calls. I’ll open to a standard 3xBB (300) — bigger than the min-raise to discourage loose calls and small squeezes but small enough that I can fold without significant loss to a 3‑bet. If I face a 3‑bet I'll fold; if called I'll use position to play postflop, c‑betting favorable textures and exercising caution on heavy action.

T8o is a weak hand to play out of position from the small blind. While Minni's button raise to 300 is standard and could be a wide range, I don't have sufficient hand strength to call and play out of position post-flop. The hand lacks connectivity, isn't a pair, and has poor potential against most of Minni's raising range. With a healthy stack (18,950) and only 52 hands played of 150, it's better to preserve chips and wait for premium hands in better positions. Folding is the optimal play here.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Six offsuit (Jc 6d). Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. While ranges are wider in 3-handed play, J6o is a very weak hand to defend out of position against a 3x open. I don't have enough equity or playability to justify calling the 200 more chips here.

Q4 offsuit is a weak hand from the button. While position is favorable, this hand doesn't meet the threshold for a profitable raise. Q4o lacks strong equity, has poor connectivity, and is easily dominated if called. Given that we're 53 hands into a 150-hand session and I have a healthy 18,950 chip stack, it's important to be selective with hand ranges and fold marginal holdings. Better opportunities will present themselves in upcoming hands.

Holding Ks7s in the Small Blind after the Button (Claude) has folded, I have a strong hand for head-up play against the Big Blind (Minni). Raising is the standard play here to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds immediately. A 3x raise to 300 is a standard sizing to put pressure on the BB while maintaining a good pot-to-stack ratio.

Holding 4♣ T♣ in the big blind facing a 3x SB open to 300. Pot is 400 and I need to put in 200 more (300 total) — pot odds require ≈25% equity. T4s is a marginal hand but playable heads-up vs a likely wide SB-opening range: it has flush potential, some straight/backdoor equity, and decent implied odds with deep stacks (my 8,275 vs Flash 12,375). 3-betting as a bluff is possible, but T4s is too weak to build a strong 3-bet/three-barrel line profitably here. I’ll call to see a flop, keep weaker hands in the pot, and proceed based on flop texture (raise or bet if I hit big or a strong draw; pot-control or fold to heavy aggression on dry boards).

On this monotone board of all clubs (Jc 3c 5c), I am out of position against the Big Blind and do not hold a club in my hand. While I was the pre-flop aggressor, this texture is quite dangerous as it hits the caller's range well (small pairs with a club, suited connectors). Checking is the most prudent play here to control the pot size and see how Minni reacts. If she checks behind, I get a free turn card; if she bets, I will likely have to fold given my lack of equity.

Flop: J♣ 3♣ 5♣ — I hold T♣4♣: a made flush but not the nut (A♣/K♣/Q♣ beat me). Flash was the preflop raiser and checked the flop, so his range is wide and includes many club combos, weaker clubs, and pairs/air that will call a bet. With deep stacks I want to extract value and charge any higher-card clubs and pair+club/overcard clubs while denying free cards that could bring a better club. Checking lets many hands see free cards and under-protects my hand. I’ll bet for value and protection about 2/3 pot to build the pot and fold out some non-club hands. If I face a large raise on this sizing I’ll strongly consider he has A♣/K♣/Q♣ and will re-evaluate (likely folding to a big shove, calling a small raise depending on sizing). Bet = 2/3 pot (≈400).

On this monotone board of all clubs (Jc 3c 5c), I am holding King-high with no club in my hand. After checking to the pre-flop caller, Minni has led out with a bet of 400 into 600. Without a club, I have very little equity to continue. I am drawing to a King which might not even be good if she has a flush or a set, and I have no backdoor flush potential. Floating out of position on such a coordinated board is a losing play. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Ten-Six of hearts. This is a decent suited hand to open with in late position to exert pressure on the blinds. Given my stack size and position, a standard 3x raise is appropriate here to either take down the blinds or play a pot in position against Minni or Claude.

I’m in the small blind with Tc3s — a weak, unsuited hand with poor playability. Flash opened to 300 in a 3-handed spot; I must call 250 into a 450 pot, which requires ~35.7% equity to break even. Tc3s will be dominated by a large portion of the button’s opening range and performs poorly out of position postflop (and the big blind can still come along). A light 3-bet bluff here is too thin given two opponents and deep stacks — I don’t want to commit chips with such a marginal holding. Fold and preserve stack to pick better spots (suited connectors, pairs, or strong broadways) to defend or apply pressure.

I hold AK (Ace-King), one of the premium starting hands in poker. Flash has raised to 300 from the button, likely with a wide range given button tendencies. With only Minni folding from the small blind, the pot is 450 and I need to call 200. This is a clear 3-bet situation. I should raise to build a pot where I'm ahead of Flash's range, establish aggression, and either win the pot preflop or play a favorable post-flop situation. A raise to 750 total (adding 650 to the current pot) is standard - approximately 2.5x Flash's raise - and applies appropriate pressure while managing my 18,850 stack appropriately. My high card strength and position advantage support this aggressive action.

I am on the button with Ten-Six of hearts. I initially opened for 300, and Claude has 3-bet from the Big Blind to 750. While Ten-Six of hearts is a playable hand in many scenarios, Claude has been playing quite aggressively and currently holds the chip lead. Calling a 3-bet out of position (if Minni had stayed in) or even in position here with a marginal hand like T6s is generally not profitable against a strong 3-bet range. Given the action and the need to preserve my stack over 150 hands, folding is the most prudent play here.

I’m on the button with Tc‑8d in a 3-handed game. In short‑handed play the button should open a fairly wide range — this hand has enough connectivity/fe-card potential to play in position. I’ll take the initiative and raise to a standard 3xBB (300) rather than limp/call; that reduces freebies, isolates the pot, and gives me the initiative post‑flop. With ~86BB effective I can continue on favourable flops and fold to a large, committed 3‑bet — I’ll fold to a big 3‑bet but will consider calling a small squeeze to see a flop in position. Post‑flop plan: c‑bet regularly on dry/advantageous textures, proceed cautiously against strong resistance or multiple tough draws.

Calling Minni's button raise with QT offsuit from the small blind. The pot odds of 1.8:1 justify the call with this hand's decent equity against a wide opening range. Position is reasonable relative to the button, and stack depths allow for postflop play. QT has both straight and pair-making potential, making it a profitable call in this spot.

I am in the big blind with 7d 5s. Minni opened to 300 from the button and Claude called from the small blind. I am getting 3.5-to-1 odds to call (200 to win 700), but 75 offsuit is a very weak hand that performs poorly multi-way and out of position. Given the high rake and the difficulty of realizing equity with such a marginal hand against two opponents, folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack.

On the flop of Ks 6h 9h, I have QT which gives me Queen-high with a gutshot straight draw (any 8 makes a straight) plus two pair possibilities. In this heads-up spot against Minni's button raise, I'm out of position, which argues for pot control. My hand has decent equity (pair outs, straight draws) and reasonable showdown value with Queen-high. Checking allows me to control the pot size, see Minni's action, and avoid building a large pot out of position with a moderate hand. The dry board (no connected cards except for the hearts) means Minni is likely to continuation bet with a significant portion of her range, giving me opportunities to call with my draws or raise if appropriate on the turn. Betting would build the pot while out of position with a vulnerable hand that may not be ahead.

I opened preflop and have position. The flop K♠-6♥-9♥ missed my T♣8♦, but it’s a fairly dry K‑high board and Claude checked — a textbook spot to continuation‑bet and use my range advantage. A small c‑bet (~300 into the 700 pot) applies pressure to overcards, weak pairs and floaters, and charges any backdoor draws while keeping the pot controllable. Plan: if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn; if he check‑raises large I’ll give up (my hand has limited showdown equity vs Kx/sets/strong draws).

Calling with QT gutshot on K-6-9 board. The pot odds of 4.33:1 are favorable against Minni's wide button range. With two cards to come, my gutshot (needing a Jack for a straight) plus Queen-high has approximately 25-28% equity, making this a profitable call. In heads-up play, I shouldn't fold too many marginal hands. Stack depths allow for future flexibility.

Checking on the turn with QT gutshot against Minni's position. The 3c is a blank for my hand, and being out of position with a marginal hand warrants pot control. I maintain flexibility to see the river card while avoiding unnecessary commitment. My gutshot still has two cards to improve, and checking keeps the pot size manageable while potentially inducing action or reaching showdown.

Board K♠ 6♥ 9♥ 3♣ — my T♣8♦ missed. After my flop c‑bet Claude called, so his range likely contains Kx, 9x, 6x, and heart draws or other middling pairs. On the turn I only have a one‑card gutshot (a 7 completes 6‑7‑8‑9‑10) — 4 outs ≈ 4/46 ≈ 8.7% to hit the river. Pot is 1,300, so calling a bet on the turn is only +EV if the bet is very small (breakeven bet ≈ 137 chips); against any meaningful turn bet I should fold. I have position and little equity, so I’ll control the pot and check. Plan: check‑fold to a sizable bet, call a very small probe (≈100) because of pot odds, and if he checks back see the river and reassess (bet for value if a 7 hits, otherwise look to take a cheap showdown or bluff selectively).

River analysis with Queen-high (no pair, no draw completion). The board Ks 6h 9h 3c 2h is a complete blank for my QT gutshot - I missed my Jack on the turn and haven't improved further. Key observation: Minni checked the turn, which strongly suggests weakness. A stronger hand like KK, KQ, or AK would likely apply pressure on the turn; her turn check indicates she's either on a bricked draw or a marginal holding. With Queen-high, I have a weak but showdownable hand that beats many of Minni's folded equity and weak holdings. The 2h river card doesn't change the dynamic. By checking, I: (1) reach showdown with a marginal hand that could easily be ahead against Minni's button range containing many air hands; (2) minimize losses if I'm beat; (3) retain the option to evaluate any river bet from Minni; (4) avoid building a larger pot out of position with a weak hand. Betting would be speculative and risk getting called by better hands. Checking is pot control with decent implied showdown value given Minni's apparent weakness on the turn.

Final board K♠ 6♥ 9♥ 3♣ 2♥. I hold T♣8♦ and missed — no hearts, no pair. Pot is 1,300. Claude called my flop c‑bet and then went passive (check on turn and river), which signals lots of Kx/9x, some weak pairs and a subset of heart draws that now completed on the river. My pure showdown equity vs that checked‑back range is low (~20–30%), and the river 2♥ reduces my fold equity vs the hands that called the flop. A small blocker bet would be marginal and a larger bluff will get called by Kx or the now‑made flushes. Better EV is to take the free showdown, preserve my stack, and learn more about his tendencies — so I check back.

Pocket Kings is a premium hand from the button against two opponents. I should raise to 3x BB (300 chips) to build the pot, establish aggression, and control the action. This is a standard opening raise that maximizes value while keeping the pot playable.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Nine offsuit. Claude has raised from the Button to 300. In a 3-handed game, A9o is a strong hand to defend against a Button open. Calling is standard here to keep the pot manageable and see a flop, though a 3-bet is also an option. Given my stack size and position, calling is a solid play that allows me to realize my equity without bloating the pot out of position against a deep-stacked aggressive opponent like Claude.

Qc2h is a very weak, offsuit hand with poor playability and lots of dominated combos versus a button raising range. Claude opened to 300 and Flash cold‑called, so this would be a 3‑way pot. To call I must add 200 into a 700 pot (making it 900) — I need ~22% equity to break even. Multiway, Q2o’s equity vs an open+call range is typically well below that and I’ll be in tough postflop spots. There's no reason to inflate the pot or risk chips with such a hand; folding preserves chips for better spots.

I have Ace-high on a paired board (Qs 7c 7d). As the Small Blind who called a pre-flop raise, the standard play is to check to the aggressor (Claude) to see how they react to this texture. My hand has some showdown value but isn't strong enough to lead out into the pre-flop raiser.

With pocket Kings on a Qs-7c-7d flop, I have an overpair that's likely the strongest hand. Flash checked to me after calling my pre-flop raise, which typically indicates weakness or a hand that wants to see cards cheaply. I have strong hand equity and position. A standard flop continuation bet of ~2/3 pot (450 chips into the 700 pot) achieves multiple objectives: (1) extracts value from weaker hands (mid-pair, ace-high, drawing hands), (2) applies pressure and potentially wins the pot immediately, (3) builds a pot I'm favored to win, and (4) doesn't overcommit my stack. The pair of 7s on the board does present some concern for a flopped set, but Flash didn't re-raise pre-flop, making this less likely. This bet size gives good risk-reward and is a standard aggressive play from position with a premium hand.

On a paired board of Qs-7c-7d, my Ace-high (Ad-9h) remains a relatively strong hand with significant showdown value in a heads-up pot against a Button raiser. Claude's continuation bet of 450 into 700 is a standard play that he would make with a wide range of hands, including total air, King-high, and straight draws. While I am behind any Queen or 7, I am ahead of many of his bluffs and have three outs to an Ace. Folding immediately would be too exploitable on such a dry board. I will call the bet and re-evaluate on the turn.

The board has paired sevens twice, meaning there are four sevens on the board (Quads). Every player now has four-of-a-kind sevens. The winner of the hand will be determined by the best fifth card (the kicker). I hold the Ace of Diamonds, which means my hand is 7-7-7-7-A. This is the absolute nuts on this board; I cannot be beaten, only tied if my opponent also holds an Ace. Since I am first to act and Claude has shown aggression on the flop, I will check to induce a bet or allow him to continue a bluff, planning to extract maximum value on the river or via a check-raise.

On the turn with three sevens now on the board (Q♠-7♣-7♦-7♥), I hold pocket kings which is still a very strong hand. Flash has checked to me after calling my flop c-bet, which indicates weakness or a marginal holding. The third 7 is an ugly card that completes trips on the board, but Flash's check suggests they don't have a 7 (which would more likely lead to a check-raise). Flash likely holds something like AK, AQ, a middle pair, or a draw that hasn't improved. My KK is well ahead of this range. Betting approximately 47% of the pot (750 into 1600) continues to apply pressure and extract value from weaker holdings while avoiding massive overcommitment. This bet size also controls the pot and gives me good information about Flash's hand strength. I'm confident my hand is currently the strongest, and this bet achieves the goals of value extraction and keeping Flash playing marginal hands.

The board currently shows four sevens (Qs-7c-7d-7h), meaning every player has four-of-a-kind. The winner is determined by the highest fifth card (the kicker). I hold the Ace of Diamonds, which is the highest possible kicker, giving me the absolute nuts (or a tie if Claude also holds an Ace). Claude has shown aggression throughout the hand, betting both the flop and the turn. If I raise now on the turn, I risk folding out his bluffs or weaker kickers (like King-high or a Queen) that might otherwise bet again on the river. By calling, I keep his range as wide as possible and plan to extract more value on the final street, either by leading out or check-raising depending on the river card and his action.

On a board of Qs-7c-7d-7h-4s, there are four-of-a-kind sevens. My hand is 7-7-7-7 with an Ace kicker (Ad), which is the absolute nuts. I cannot be beaten, only tied if Claude also holds an Ace. Claude has been the aggressor throughout the hand, betting the flop and the turn. On the river, I want to ensure value is extracted. While checking might induce a bluff, there is a risk Claude checks back a Queen or a lower kicker out of caution. By betting, I target his entire range—including Queens, pocket pairs, and King-high kickers—that might call a single bet thinking they are splitting the pot or catching a bluff on such a dry, paired board. I will bet 2,000, which is roughly two-thirds of the pot, to maximize value from his non-Ace hands.

Flash's check-call line through three streets followed by a river raise is suspicious and more consistent with a bluff or marginal hand than a strong holding. With pocket Kings on a trip-seven board, I'm only beaten by a 7 or strong Queen. Flash's passive line through the turn makes them unlikely to have a 7 (which would have check-raised earlier). I have 4.5:1 pot odds and need only ~30% equity to call. Given Flash's weak action pattern and my premium hand strength, calling is the mathematically and strategically sound play. Flash is likely trying to win the pot with an underpair, ace-high, or a bluff.

I am on the button with a premium hand, a pocket pair of Queens (Qc Qd). In a three-handed game, this is an extremely strong starting hand. My goal is to build a pot while the blinds are relatively small. A standard open-raise of 3x the big blind is appropriate here to extract value and potentially thin the field, although in a 3-way game, I'm happy to play against one or both opponents. I will raise to 300.

3♠8♠ is a very weak hand — low and poorly connected despite being suited. Flash opened to 300; I’d have to call 250 out of the small blind and will be out of position with the big blind still to act. Calling often leads to a multiway pot where my equity and realizable outs are poor. A 3‑bet bluff here is possible in some spots but with the big blind deep behind and both opponents capable of calling or 4‑betting, it’s too risky and unlikely to be +EV. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

Big blind defense with K7o. Pot odds of 2.25:1 justify a call with ~31-35% equity. K7o has reasonable equity against Flash's likely button range. Calling allows me to see the flop and make postflop decisions. If I fold too often from the BB, Flash's button steals become too profitable.

Checking back K7o on Ac 6c 2d. Flash's preflop raise likely connected with the ace, and I have minimal equity with only king-high and a backdoor flush draw. Being out of position and facing weakness (Flash's check), checking keeps the pot small with a marginal hand and allows me to see the turn at minimal cost. This is a disciplined big blind defense spot where I don't force the issue.

I have pocket Queens on an Ace-high board. Claude checked to me. This board is quite good for the pre-flop caller (Claude) if they have an Ace, and as the pre-flop aggressor, I have to be cautious. However, I still have a very strong hand (second pair) and there are flush draws (two clubs) and potential straight draws. I will place a continuation bet to test Claude's hand and protect my equity against draws. A bet of about half the pot (325) is standard here to see where I stand.

K7o is a marginal hand on an Ac 6c 2d flop where Flash has bet into my check. Against Flash's likely range (which probably includes many aces given the button raise and flop aggression), K7o has approximately 25-28% equity - insufficient to justify the call against the 325 bet. While I'm getting 3:1 pot odds (975:325), the equity is borderline at best, and I'm out of position facing an aggressive opponent who will have strong continuation betting incentives. With a deep stack (23,300), I don't need to force marginal situations with undercards. This is a disciplined fold on a flop where my king-high has minimal showdown value and poor equity against Flash's range.

Button with Q♥9♣ — marginal offsuit but very playable in position against only the blinds. With pot 150 and 100 to call, I want to take the initiative and open a standard button raise to 300 (≈3×BB) to steal the blinds and play postflop in position. Claude (SB) has shown aggression and Flash (BB) defends; I’m prepared to fold Q9o to a large 3‑bet but will continue vs calls or small squeezes depending on pot odds and stack depth. Raising here is the highest EV line: it wins many pots preflop and gives me position to realize equity or apply pressure postflop.

Holding 4-3 offsuit (the worst hand in poker) from the small blind. Minni has raised to 300 from the button. While I'm only laying 250 to call and the pot odds are approximately 1.8:1, this hand has minimal equity against a typical raising range. Even with favorable pot odds, 4-3o lacks the hand strength to justify continuing - it has virtually no showdown value and minimal potential to win the hand. This is a clear fold situation. Flash still has to act behind me, which could lead to further aggression. Folding weak hands out of position allows me to play stronger hands more profitably, especially in early stages of a 150-hand tournament.

I am in the Big Blind with 8s 6h. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. Although the pot odds are roughly 3.25 to 1, 86o is a weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive button opening range. Given my current stack and the relatively high risk of being outplayed post-flop with a marginal hand, I will choose to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

With 3s9h on the button, this is a marginal hand that doesn't justify continued investment despite my positional advantage. While I've been playing aggressively throughout this session, 3-9 offsuit lacks the connectivity and card quality needed to profitably play out of position post-flop against two opponents. The hand is too weak to win at showdown if called, and even from the button, I should reserve my aggression for hands with better equity. At 23,250 chips with 91 hands remaining, I can afford to be selective and wait for premium stealing opportunities with better hand strength.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Four suited (Ah 4h). After the Button folds, it is just me and the Big Blind. Ah 4h is a strong hand in a blind-versus-blind situation. Raising is the standard play here to either win the pot immediately or build a pot with a hand that has good post-flop playability and equity. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind).

Call 300. Pot is 400 and I only need to put in 200 more (pot odds ~3:1) so defending a marginal hand cheaply is correct. Q♠2♦ is weak and often dominated, so 3‑betting out of position is unnecessary and too risky; I’ll fold to a sizable 3‑bet. Plan: see a flop cheaply and proceed only with real equity (top pair, two pair, trips or strong draws); otherwise fold to significant post‑flop aggression to protect my stack and preserve fold equity for later hands.

On a flop of 7s-8s-7c, the board is paired and connected. My hand, Ah 4h, has no direct connection to this board other than being an overcard with some backdoor flush potential (though not the same suit as the board's spades). Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and this board texture often misses a defender's range or hits it in ways that make them sticky (like 7x or 8x), I should test the waters with a continuation bet. A small bet of about half the pot (300) is effective here to put pressure on air hands and potentially win the pot immediately without over-committing.

The flop is 7s-8s-7c and Flash (preflop raiser) made a 300 continuation bet into a 900 pot. I hold Qs2d — no pair, but a spade blocker and a backdoor spade possibility. Calling 300 requires ~25% equity (300 / (900+300)), and the call is a very small portion of my 7,725 stack. Flash’s range as the preflop raiser will include a lot of overcards and many hands that will c-bet (some air, some Kx/Ax, and some pocket pairs like 88/77), so Q-high will have enough showdown equity against his continuation range to justify a cheap defend. Plan: call the 300 to see the turn, then fold to significant turn aggression if I don’t improve (or if the turn completes obvious draws and he bets strongly). If I hit a Q or a spade on the turn I’ll proceed more aggressively; otherwise I’ll be willing to let this go to protect my stack.

On the turn, the board is 7s-8s-7c-5s. The five of spades is a very wet card, completing both the flush and potential straight draws (like 6-4 or 9-6). Since Minni called my flop bet on a paired board, her range is likely composed of 8x hands, 7x hands, pocket pairs, or spade draws. My Ah 4h now has very little equity against that range, and continuing to bet into a range that has likely improved or is willing to call down is suboptimal.
