I will check to see how Minni responds and try to get to a cheap showdown or fold if she bets heavily.

I hold Q♠2♦ on 7♠-8♠-7♣-5♠. The turn completed a single-card flush draw for me (I now have 4 spades including Q♠). Flash checked after c-betting the flop — a good opportunity to semi-bluff. My Q♠ is a useful blocker to nut-spade combos and many hands in his flop-c-bet range (Kx/Ax, missed broadways, some small pairs) will fold to a turn bet. If called I have ~19.6% to hit the flush on the river (9 spades/46) and ~26% to hit either a Queen or a spade (12 outs/46), so betting to build the pot when I hit and to get immediate fold equity is correct. I will size modestly to ~1/2 pot to apply pressure but preserve stack flexibility. If he raises significantly I will usually fold — this is a semi-bluff, not a committed value hand.

On a very connected board of 7s-8s-7c-5s, the flush and straight draws have completed. My hand (Ah 4h) is merely Ace-high with no spades and no straight potential. Minni called my pre-flop raise and flop continuation bet, indicating she has a piece of the board or a draw. Her 600 bet into an 1800 pot on the turn after I checked suggests she either has a completed hand (like a flush, 7x, 8x, or a straight) or is betting to protect her equity. Given my lack of equity and the likelihood that she is ahead, calling or raising is not profitable. I will fold.

I am on the button with 5c 2s, which is a very weak hand. In a three-handed game, while I want to be aggressive, this hand is at the bottom of the range and doesn't warrant a steal attempt or a call against the blinds. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

2♣4♠ is essentially junk and plays poorly out of position, but the price to see a flop is tiny (50 into a 150 pot). Raising here would be a pure bluff that Claude (a capable, often-sticky raiser) is likely to call or 3-bet, so I don’t want to inflate the pot. Folding is a bit too tight given the pot odds. I’ll call the 50 to see a flop cheaply, then proceed very cautiously OOP — only continue if I pair a 2 or 4 or pick up strong draws; otherwise check-fold to significant aggression.

Minni limped from SB with likely a weak hand. K7 suited is above average and I have chip advantage (23k vs 8.5k). Raising to 300 (3x BB) applies pressure, builds the pot with a solid holding, and exploits the weak limp. This is a standard aggressive play in my profitable style.

2♣4♠ is junk and plays poorly out of position, but the raise to 300 after I completed is cheap. Calling 200 into a ~400 pot gives ~33% pot‑odds — folding is too tight and a 3‑bet would be a pure bluff versus a sticky, capable raiser (Claude). I’ll call to see a cheap flop and proceed very cautiously OOP: if I don’t hit a pair (2 or 4) or a very strong draw I’ll check‑fold to meaningful continuation bets or raises. Preserve stack and don’t inflate the pot without a real hand.

I called preflop cheaply intending to proceed very cautiously OOP. The flop A♣‑7♥‑5♦ completely misses my 2♣4♠ — no pair, no meaningful draw (only a remote runner‑runner club). Claude was the preflop raiser and is a capable, often‑sticky aggressor who will C‑bet or barrel many strong hands here, so I don’t want to inflate the pot with air. Check and fold to any significant bet; if everyone checks through I’ll reassess the turn only if I pick up real equity (a pair or a strong draw).

I'm holding 7s Ks with a pair of sevens on a flop of 7h 5d Ac. Minni limped from the SB, called my 300 pre-flop raise, and just checked on the flop—indicating weakness. With a pair and King kicker in a good spot (large chip stack, in position, aggressive image), this is a textbook continuation bet. The flop has an Ace which could concern me, but Minni's check suggests they either missed it or have a weak ace at best. A 400 bet (roughly 65% of the 600 pot) is appropriately sized to build the pot when I'm likely ahead, maintain momentum from my pre-flop aggression, and apply pressure on a weaker hand. This is consistent with my profitable aggressive style throughout the session.

Completely missed the flop with 2♣4♠ on A♣‑7♥‑5♦. Claude was the pre‑flop raiser and now c‑bet to 400 into a 1000 pot — a sizable bet representing many hands that beat me (A‑x, sets, two‑pair, overpairs). Calling 400 to win 1400 requires ≈28.6% equity. My realistic equity to improve (pair a 2 or 4 on the turn or river) is only about 24% (6 outs over two cards), so calling is a negative‑EV decision. I’m out of position, have almost no fold equity and no strong redraws, so the best play is to preserve my stack for a better spot and fold.

On the button with 6♦4♠ in a 3-handed spot: this is a marginal holding but position is huge and the blinds are small relative to my stack. Folding is too nitty and limping surrenders initiative and fold equity. I want to open a relatively wide stealing range here. I’ll raise to 300 (a standard ~3xBB open) — it’s a small % of my 8,325 stack so the risk is low but it maximizes fold equity and makes postflop play easier in position. If I get called I’ll c-bet on favorable, low-card flops and proceed cautiously (check/fold) on heavy or overcarded boards; fold to a large 3-bet unless I pick up decent equity. Raising here is the highest EV play.

Calling 250 to contest a 450 pot with 8s7s. While this is a marginal hand, the pot odds (1.8:1) are acceptable against Minni's wide button-raising range. The hand has playable equity through spade draws and wheel potential. In a 3-handed game with aggressive button raising, I should defend the small blind with reasonable hands. If Flash folds, I'm heads-up with positional advantage; if Flash calls, I can evaluate my flop equity with potential draws.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Jack suited, which is a premium hand in a three-handed game. Minni opened from the Button and Claude called from the Small Blind. This is a perfect opportunity for a squeeze play to either take down the pot immediately or build a larger pot with a significant range advantage against two wide ranges. I will raise to 1200, which is roughly 4 times the initial raise, to put pressure on both opponents and extract value.

Fold. I opened to 300 but facing Flash’s 3‑bet to 1,200 I’d have to call 900 into a 1,800 pot (≈33% equity). 6♦4♠ has very poor equity vs a standard 3‑bet range (broadways, pairs, some suited connectors) and will be even worse if Claude remains in, making this effectively a multiway spot. A light 4‑bet shove or min‑reraise with this holding is too spewy and easily exploited. I earlier committed to folding to large 3‑bets without decent equity — I’ll preserve my stack and wait for a better spot.

Calling 900 more (1200 total commitment) with 8s7s against Flash's 3-bet. Pot odds of 2:1 are favorable for a speculative hand with multiple draw possibilities. The hand has good equity against a wide big blind 3-betting range, especially against Minni's loose button raise. Deep stacks allow for strong post-flop play where I can leverage my superior position. Flash's relatively short stack also makes the pot equity more valuable.

8s7s on 4c Qc 3h is a strong draw with ~15+ outs (flush draw, wheel draws, straight potential). With Flash's check from the big blind 3-bet, I should capitalize on fold equity while maintaining strong semi-bluff equity. The 1500 bet (~55% pot) builds value efficiently and exploits Flash's checked weakness. Deep stacks and position support aggressive play with quality draws.

I 3-bet pre-flop with Ace-Jack of clubs and Claude called from the Small Blind. The flop is Queen-high with two clubs, giving me the nut flush draw and one overcard (the Ace). Claude has led out for 1500 into a 2700 pot. This 'donk bet' after calling a 3-bet often indicates a strong hand like top pair or a set, but could also be a medium-strength hand trying to define its position. With my nut flush draw and overcard, I have approximately 35-40% equity against most of his value range. The pot odds (calling 1500 to win a total pot of 5700) are roughly 26%, so a call is mathematically sound. Calling allows me to realize my equity and potentially trap him if I hit my flush on the turn, while keeping the pot manageable if I miss.

Turn check with 8s7s on 4c Qc 3h 9h. I have ~15 clean outs to the river (9 spade flush outs, 3 non-spade 5s for wheel, 3 non-spade 6s for straight), giving me roughly 32% equity. Flash checked, indicating weakness or caution. With his short stack (5,425 chips) and the pot already at 5,700, taking a free river card is high EV. Flash's flop call suggests a made hand that likely won't fold another turn bet, so the free card is more valuable than fold equity. I'll extract value on the river based on my hits and final board texture.

On the turn, Claude has checked after leading the flop. I have the nut flush draw and one overcard (the Ace). While I could bet to put pressure on his marginal Queens or straight draws, checking back is the most strategic play here. It allows me to realize my equity for free and ensures I see the river. My stack is roughly equal to the pot size, so checking behind prevents me from being forced to make a difficult decision against a potential check-raise while I'm still on a draw. If I hit my club or an Ace on the river, I am in a great position to extract value or win the pot.

River decision with 8s7s on 4c Qc 3h 9h Td. I have Q-high (Queen-Ten-Nine-Eight-Seven), a marginal hand. Flash called my flop bet and checked the turn, suggesting he has a made hand worth protecting. Betting Q-high into his likely stronger hand is marginal. Checking allows me to reach showdown while preserving fold equity - Flash might check back with weak holdings or missed draws, and my Q-high plays well in a showdown against complete misses. With limited fold equity on the river and a hand that doesn't improve, checking is the highest EV play.

I missed my nut flush draw and ended the hand with Ace-high. Claude's line is interesting: he donk-bet the flop, then checked both the turn and the river. This usually indicates a hand with marginal showdown value (like a weak Queen or a middle pair) that he didn't want to play a huge pot with, or a draw that he has given up on. Because I checked behind on the turn, a bluff on the river would look very much like a missed draw, which is exactly what I have. Claude is unlikely to fold even a weak pair given the pot size and my perceived range. Ace-high has a small chance of winning at showdown against other missed draws (like 56 of clubs or hearts), but I will lose to any pair. I'll check back and preserve my remaining stack for a better opportunity.

Pocket threes on the button is a solid raising hand against the short-stacked blinds. With a ~20k stack, I have good initiative and fold equity. A standard 3x BB raise (300) accomplishes multiple objectives: (1) wins the blinds and dead money preflop if both fold, (2) builds a pot where I can realize equity postflop with position, and (3) asserts control from the button. Both opponents have relatively short stacks (Flash ~11k, Minni ~7.9k), making them more vulnerable to positional pressure. Small pairs play well either by winning preflop or by set mining when we get reasonable odds postflop with position. This is better than calling, which is too passive for button position.

I am holding 62o in the Small Blind. This is one of the weakest starting hands in poker and has very little playability post-flop, especially out of position against a button raiser like Claude. The pot odds of 1.8:1 are not nearly enough to justify a call with such a low-equity hand. I will fold.

I’m in the big blind with A♦9♦ facing a standard 3bb open to 300 from the button (heads-up after the SB folded). A9s is too good to fold heads-up and it plays well postflop (nut‑flush potential, decent top‑pair hand). However I’m out of position postflop, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal 3‑bet; a polarized 3‑bet or a small 3‑bet is unnecessary here. Calling keeps the pot controlled, realizes equity and lets me play flops with a hand that fares well against a wide opening range. Pot math: I need 200 more into a 450 pot (200/(450+200) ≈ 30.8%), which is a reasonable price to see a flop. Plan: call to 300, evaluate the flop — continue with top pair or strong draws, fold to heavy action when missed; only 3‑bet this spot versus a specific exploitable opponent or for balance, and fold to a large 4‑bet.

Heads-up on 5♥ 8♠ 9♠ and I hold A♦9♦ — top pair with the best possible kicker but the board has two spades, so my hand is vulnerable to flush draws. Claude opened from the button, so his range is wide and includes many spade draws, worse pairs (9x, 8x, 5x) and single-pair hands. Checking hands like this OOP gives free equity to spade draws and surrenders initiative. I want to both get value from worse pairs and charge / deny equity to spade draws, while keeping the pot controllable out of position. A standard value/protection bet of roughly half the pot is appropriate. Bet 325 into 650 (~50% pot). Plan: if called, re-evaluate on the turn; if I face a large check‑raise I’ll give credit to a strong made hand (flush/set/9x) and proceed cautiously (fold to very large polarizing raises, consider calling smaller aggression depending on sizing and reads).

Minni's min-raise on this flop with pocket threes merits careful consideration. The board (5h-8s-9s) offers me limited equity as-is - my pair is dominated by many of her likely holdings (any piece of the board, overcards, etc.). However, several factors support calling: (1) She min-raised, which is a weak action that suggests she's either testing or has moderate strength rather than a premium hand. (2) I'm getting ~5:1 pot odds (975+325 to call 325), and I have roughly 17-18% equity when accounting for set mining (8-10% to hit a set plus some occasional wins with the pair) plus future fold equity from position. (3) With position on the button, I can control the action on turn/river - if I hit my set (2 clean outs), I have a powerful hand; if I miss and she continues aggressive, I can fold. (4) My stack depth (20k) allows me to take a speculative call here. Raising doesn't make sense given my hand strength, and folding gives up too much equity to a likely weak flop bet. Calling best balances pot odds, position, and potential upside.

I have A♦9♦ on a 5♥ 8♠ 9♠ T♦ board — pair of nines with the best kicker, out of position vs Claude. I bet the flop (325) and he called, so his range contains single pairs (5s, 8s, 9x), spade draws, some broadways and connected hands. The T♦ on the turn is a somewhat hostile card: it gives additional two‑pair/10x combos and completes 6‑7 straights, but most importantly checking now would give free cards to spade/straight draws and surrender initiative. I want to charge those draws and extract value from worse one‑pair hands while keeping the pot manageable. Pot is 1,300; a bet around 2/3 pot (~900) denies drawing hands good pot odds (an opponent must call 900 to win ~3,100, ≈29% equity) and still gets value from worse pairs and some turned tens. If Claude calls I’ll re‑evaluate the river; if he check‑raises big he’s likely to have a set/flush/strong two‑pair/straight and I’ll fold to large polarizing aggression. Bet 900 to protect and extract value.

Folding pocket threes on the turn. The board (5h-8s-9s-Td) is highly connected and likely hit Minni's range (probable straights, sets, or two pairs). My equity is approximately 5-7% (2 outs to a set), well below the 29% needed for the 2.44:1 pot odds. The turn card improved many holdings while my hand remains weak. With limited fold equity and no strong draws, this is a clear mathematical fold. Minni's escalating aggression (min-raise flop → bigger raise turn) suggests a legitimate hand rather than a bluff.

I am on the button with KQs, a premium starting hand. I want to raise to build the pot and take the initiative as the aggressor. A standard raise size for me throughout this session has been around 300, and there is no reason to change that here.

Holding A♣J♣ in the SB facing a button open to 300 (pot 450, 250 to call). AJs is a strong, playable hand with great blockers (blocks many A–x and club combinations) and good post‑flop playability. Flash’s open from the button looks like a standard, often fairly wide range, and Claude (BB) will defend a lot, so a simple flat call risks playing a multiway pot OOP with a hand that wants to isolate and take the lead. I want fold equity and to thin the field while still keeping a sensible stack behind, so I’ll 3‑bet to a size that pressures the opener but isn’t committing — raise to 1000 (total). That sizing (~3.3x the open, ~11–12% of my stack) gives good chance to isolate the button, extracts value from worse Ax and broadway combos, and protects against multiway scenarios. Plan post‑flop: continue as the preflop aggressor (c‑bet favorable textures, pot control on dry boards) and re‑evaluate if I face a sizable 4‑bet.

2-8 offsuit is one of the worst starting hands in poker with minimal equity and poor playability. I'm out of position in the big blind facing a 3-bet situation where Minni has shown significant strength by re-raising Flash's raise to 1000. While calling 900 only represents about 4.5% of my 20,125 stack, it's a mathematically poor decision with such a weak hand. Even though I have decent remaining depth and the pot odds (calling 900 into 1400), the hand itself is indefensible out of position against two opponents showing aggression. This is a straightforward fold.

I opened to 300 with KQs on the button, and Minni 3-bet to 1000 from the small blind. KQs is a very strong hand that plays well post-flop, especially in position. While 4-betting is an option, calling allows me to keep Minni's range wider and utilize my positional advantage. The pot odds (700 to call into a pot of 1400) are favorable, and I have the stack depth to play post-flop.

I have A♣J♣ on J♦6♦7♦ — top pair with the best available kicker vs Jx. The flop is monotone and contains diamond draws, but I block A♦ combos which reduces some of villain’s strongest diamond+ace draws. Flash called my 3‑bet preflop, so his range is wide (broadways, medium pairs, suited connectors and diamond draws). I want value and to deny free cards to draws while not bloating an OOP pot, so I’ll make a ~1/2‑pot continuation bet to charge flush/straight draws and get called by worse Jx and mid pairs. Bet 1000 into 2100: if called I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn (pot‑control or target small bets on non‑diamond cards; be cautious if a diamond or heavy raise appears).

I have flopped the second-nut flush (King-high) on a monotone board. My hand is extremely strong, only losing to the Ace-high flush. Minni showed strength pre-flop with a 3-bet and is now continuing with a bet of 1000. By calling instead of raising, I keep her bluffs and overpairs without a diamond in her range, allowing her to continue her aggression on the turn. Since I am in position and the effective stacks are around 67bb, I can comfortably call here and look to extract more value on later streets while keeping the pot manageable if she happens to hold the Ace of diamonds.

I have A♣J♣ — top pair with the best kicker — but the flop (J♦6♦7♦) is very wet: Flash called my flop c‑bet so his range contains hands that already beat me (two‑diamond flushes) as well as worse Jx and medium pairs. The turn K♣ is an off‑diamond blank that actually helps some of his calling range (it gives Kx hands top pair) and does not remove the diamond threat. Betting here simply bloats the pot and gets value mainly from hands that now beat me (Kx) or from pairs I already beat, while also getting called by flushes I can’t beat — that’s the wrong spot to force action. I’ll check to control the pot and force Flash to show strength. Plan: call a reasonable small/medium bet (I’ll re‑evaluate/shore up fold equity if he barrels large), fold to a polarizing raise (likely a made flush or set), and if he checks back we go to the river with options to extract thin value or induce bluffs on non‑diamond rivers.

I have the second-best possible hand (King-high flush) and have also paired my King on the turn. Minni's check after being the aggressor pre-flop and on the flop suggests she might be slowing down with an overpair (like Aces or Queens with a high diamond) or she could be trapping with the Ace-high flush. However, I cannot afford to check back and miss value from her calling range (sets, two pair, or a single high diamond). A bet of 2000 (roughly half pot) is large enough to build the pot for a potential river shove while keeping her range wide enough to call with marginal hands.

I checked the turn to control the size of the pot and force Flash to show strength. His 2,000 turn bet into the 4,100 flop pot (making the pot 6,100) is a medium-sized probe. I have A♣J♣ — top pair with the best kicker — which is ahead of a large chunk of his flop-calling range (worse Jx, many medium pairs and missed draws) and mainly behind made diamond flushes, sets or two‑pair Kx combinations. Calling 2,000 to see the river (final pot would be ~8,100) requires only ~25% equity, which my hand comfortably has versus his continuing range. Raising would risk committing too much versus potential diamond flushes and polarize my range unnecessarily; folding is too weak with top pair/top kicker. I will call 2,000 and re-evaluate on the river — fold to a polarizing shove or big bet on a diamond river, but extract thin value or call small bets on non‑diamond rivers.

I have A♣J♣ and the river (A♠) gives me two pair — Aces and Jacks. That’s a very strong hand vs the wide range Flash showed (preflop open, call of a 3‑bet, flop call, turn bet) but it’s still vulnerable to any diamond flush (made on the J♦6♦7♦ flop) and to rare full houses/trips. I checked the K♣ on the turn to control the pot and force him to show strength; the river A improves my hand significantly and is a good spot to extract thin value from Kx, worse Jx and other pairs/missed draws that will call a modest bet. I don’t want to bloat the pot and get raised off by a diamond flush, so I’ll make a small-to-medium value bet (~30% pot) to get called by worse and deny some bluffs. If he raises big or jams (a polarizing line), I’ll fold — that line is likely a flush or a full house that beats me.

I hold the second-nut flush (King-high) with Kd Qd on a board of Jd 6d 7d Kc As. There are no pairs on the board, so the only hand that beats me is the Ace-high flush (Ad). Minni 3-bet pre-flop and has shown significant strength throughout the hand, including a lead on the flop and a lead on the river after check-calling the turn. Given the pot size and the strength of my hand, I cannot fold. Furthermore, Minni's remaining stack is small enough that I should raise to put her all-in to extract maximum value from her likely sets, two pairs, or smaller flushes. If she has the Ad, I will lose, but the 2nd nuts in a 3-bet pot is a mandatory stack-off situation here.

Board J♦-6♦-7♦-K♣-A♠; I hold A♣J♣ — two pair (Aces & Jacks). Flash’s line (open, call 3‑bet, call flop, bet turn 2k, then raise my 2.5k river bet to 4.7k) is polarized toward made diamond flushes and sets/trips but will also include Kx, single pairs and missed floats that I currently beat. The pot is 15,300 and the additional call is 2,200, so I only need ~12.6% equity to break even (2,200 / (15,300+2,200) ≈ 12.6%). Two pair on this river has far more than ~13% equity versus a realistic raising range even with many diamond combos in it, so folding would be too tight. I’m committing my remaining stack to a cheap call rather than folding a strong showdown hand — I call the 2,200 and go to showdown.

2-3 offsuit is one of the weakest possible hands in poker. While I only need to call 50 chips more, I would be playing out of position post-flop against the big blind with a marginal hand at best. Given that I've built a solid 20,075 chip stack through disciplined play and good hand selection, there's no reason to gamble with trash hands. I'll fold and wait for better opportunities.

I am on the button with Queen-Nine suited, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Standard strategy is to raise to put pressure on the big blind and take control of the pot. A 3x raise to 300 is a standard open from this position.

I'm in the Big Blind with Jc-5h, a weak off-suit hand. Flash raised to 300 from the button/small blind position. While I'm getting decent pot odds (2:1, needing ~33% equity), J-5o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. Against Flash's likely wide raising range from the button, my hand is vulnerable and difficult to play post-flop. The 100 chips already in the pot (my big blind) are already committed, so I should not chase with a weak hand. Folding preserves my stack for premium positions where I can play stronger hands with positional advantage. This is a disciplined fold that helps maintain a solid win rate over the long run.

Calling 50 more chips with 2d4d on the button in heads-up. The pot odds are favorable (3:1), and positional advantage in heads-up play justifies calling marginal hands. Total commitment: 100 chips (50 SB + 50 call).

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Seven offsuit. Claude has limped from the Small Blind. Given the hand strength and position, checking to see a free flop is the most standard play. There's no reason to raise and bloat the pot with a marginal hand out of position.

In a limped pot, I am in the Big Blind holding Queen-Seven offsuit. The flop of 8d-5h-Js does not connect with my hand at all. Claude, who limped from the Small Blind, has checked to me. Since I have no pair and no real draw, there is no reason to bet and risk being check-raised or called when I have such low equity. I will check back to see a free turn card.

With 2d-4d on a flop of 8d-5h-Js, I currently have no made hand but do have a flush draw (9-10 outs with the remaining diamonds). Flash checked, indicating either weakness or patience. In heads-up play, taking a free card with a marginal drawing hand is standard when given the opportunity. Checking allows me to potentially hit my flush on the turn (roughly 35% equity overall with 2 cards to come). If I whiff on the turn and Flash bets, I can fold. If I improve, I can generate value. Betting here with no made hand would be speculative and risk facing a re-raise when I'm vulnerable. The check is the prudent play to control the pot size while maintaining fold equity.

On the turn, the board has paired the eight. Claude has checked for the second time in this limped pot, indicating significant weakness. While my Queen-high has very little showdown value, checking back allows me to see a free river card and potentially improve or win if Claude has an even weaker hand. Betting here might represent a Jack or an Eight, but since the pot is so small, I prefer to check and take the free card.

Flash checked the turn, indicating weakness. I'm holding 2d-4d on a board of 8d-5h-Js-8h.

Hand analysis:
- Flush draw: 10 outs (any diamond not yet seen)
- Straight draws: 3 or 6 completes a straight (5-high with a 3, 6-high with a 6)
- Total outs: ~16 (some overlap with diamonds)
- Equity with one card to come: ~36%

Decision rationale:
While my earlier assessment suggested checking to control pot size, Flash's check on the turn reveals weakness and changes the dynamic. With a drawing hand in heads-up play, I have better expected value betting here because:

1. **Fold equity**: Flash's check is weakness. A bet likely wins the pot immediately 35-40% of the time
2. **Drawing equity**: With 16 outs, I have 36% equity on the river if called
3. **Pot odds**: Betting 100 into a 200 pot (2:1 odds offered) is well-balanced between winning immediately and having showdown equity if called

EV calculation of betting 100:
- If Flash folds (35%): +100 net (win 200 pot, minus my 100 bet)
- If Flash calls (65%): I have 36% equity for 400 pot = +44 average
- Total EV: 35 + 28.6 = +63.6

Checking would give me worse expected value as Flash would likely bet the river (forcing a fold) or I'd reach showdown with nearly zero made-hand value.

Bet sizing: 100 is half the pot—aggressive enough to apply pressure but not so large that I overcommit with a draw.

In this limped pot, the board has paired on the turn (8d-5h-Js-8h). Claude has bet 100 into a 200 pot after checking the flop. While the price to call is small, my Queen-high has very little equity against even a modest range. I have no draws and limited showdown value. Continuing here would likely require a bluff on the river or hitting a Queen, both of which are low-probability outcomes. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind (Button) in a heads-up situation with Jack-Five suited. This is a standard opening hand in position to apply pressure and either win the blinds or play a pot with a range advantage and positional advantage. I will raise to 300.

Folding 2-3 offsuit from the big blind. This is the worst hand in poker with no real showdown value or playability. Against a button raise, defending with 2-3o is unprofitable both pre-flop and post-flop. Standard fold.

Heads-up from the button with 5d Ad. Aggressive raise to 200 applies pressure, takes down the pot immediately in many cases, and maintains position equity. In heads-up poker, positional aggression with reasonable hands like ace-five is standard. If called, I still have decent equity with ace-high and flush draw potential.

I am in the Big Blind with 6d 2d. Claude has raised to 200 from the Small Blind. Although I am getting 3:1 odds to call, 62 suited is a very weak hand and does not play well post-flop, especially out of position. I'll fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Jh 9h, which is a strong suited semi-connector in a heads-up situation. My opponent Claude is in the big blind. Raising here is the standard aggressive play to take control of the pot and potentially win it pre-flop, or set up a post-flop play with a hand that has good playability.

Flash has raised to 300 from the button/small blind in a heads-up situation. I'm facing a 200 call to win a 400 chip pot (2:1 odds). With 8cTc (eight-ten suited), I have a reasonable hand for heads-up play with decent playability - it can make straights, flushes, and pairs. The small effective raise (only 100 more than the blind) makes this a natural call. While I'm out of position postflop, 8-10 suited is playable enough to see a flop and make decisions based on how it hits. Folding here would be overly tight for the heads-up game, and reraising with a marginal hand isn't optimal. A call allows me to continue with good pot odds.

Pair of tens on an A-K-T flop with two hearts is decent but vulnerable. Out of position, checking allows me to control the pot size and see Flash's action before committing more chips. This is prudent heads-up strategy with a medium-strength hand that could be crushed or ahead depending on Flash's holdings. Checking invites Flash to bet marginal hands or air, providing better information for the turn decision.

I have a very strong drawing hand on an Ad-Kh-Th board. With Jh-9h, I have both a flush draw (needing one more heart) and a gutshot straight draw to the nut straight (needing any Queen). Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and Claude checked to me, a continuation bet is the standard play here. This allows me to build the pot for my draws, potentially take the pot down immediately if Claude missed, and maintain the initiative. A bet of 400 into a 600 pot (approx 2/3 pot) is a standard sizing for this texture.

Raising to 1000 total with 8cTc on AKTh board. I have a pair of tens with significant drawing potential (flush and gutshot straight outs - approximately 30-35% equity). Raising accomplishes several goals: it regains the initiative from out-of-position, puts pressure on Flash's range (especially bluffs and marginal hands), and allows me to define the pot size rather than play passively out of position facing another street bet. The raise is neither too timid nor overcommitted given my hand strength and stack depth.

On this flop of Ad-Kh-Th, I have a massive drawing hand with both a flush draw and a gutshot to the nut straight (any Queen). Claude's check-raise to 1000 indicates strength, likely a made hand like two pair, a set, or perhaps a straight already. However, with 12 outs (9 hearts and the 3 remaining Queens), I have roughly 45-48% equity against most of his range. The pot odds are also favorable; I need to call 600 more into a pot that will be 2600, which is about 23% required equity. Folding is not an option given the strength of my draw. While I could re-raise, calling allows me to realize my equity more safely and potentially trap him if I hit my draw on the turn, while keeping the pot size somewhat controlled in case the board pairs or I miss.

Betting 1200 on the turn maintains my aggressive momentum established on the flop. With a pair of tens and multiple draws (gutshot straight, flush draw), I have sufficient hand equity while also applying pressure to Flash's range. In heads-up play, continuing with this aggressive line wins the pot immediately against marginal hands and draws while keeping my image strong. The bet size is measured (46% of pot) and allows room for river decisions without overcommitting my 18,775 stack.

On the turn, the board is Ad-Kh-Th-9c. I hold Jh-9h, giving me bottom pair and a massive combo draw. Specifically, I have a flush draw (9 outs: Ah, Qh, 8h, 7h, 6h, 5h, 4h, 3h, 2h) and a gutshot straight draw (3 additional outs for the Broadway straight: Qd, Qc, Qs; the Qh is already counted). This gives me approximately 12 outs to a very strong hand. Claude's line of check-raising the flop and leading the turn for 1200 indicates extreme strength, likely two pair, a set, or even a made straight (QJ). However, with the pot currently at 3800 and needing to call 1200, I am getting pot odds of 3.16-to-1 (about 24% equity required). My probability of hitting one of my 12 outs on the river is roughly 26%. Given the favorable pot odds and significant implied odds if I hit my draw against Claude's likely strong range, calling is the most profitable decision. Raising would be risky against such a strong line, and folding would be a mistake given the equity.

River check with pair of tens on AKT9 5-runout. Flash checked after calling my flop raise and turn bet, indicating likely weakness. My pair of tens is strong enough to win this showdown against marginal holdings (QJ straight is the main concern, but less likely given action). Checking avoids the risk of a check-raise on the river while still winning the pot against Flash's probable marginal hands or air. This is a value-maximizing decision that takes the showdown with a hand that's likely best.

On the river, the board is Ad-Kh-Th-9c-5h and I hold Jh-9h, which gives me a King-high flush (K-J-T-9-5). This is a very strong hand, especially considering the board texture. Claude's line of check-raising the flop and betting the turn suggests a very strong range, such as a straight (QJ), sets (AA, KK, TT), or top two pair (AK). When the third heart (5h) lands on the river and Claude checks, it likely indicates that he is wary of the flush completing. While the Ah and Qh are still out there and could beat me, Claude would likely bet the nut flush (AhXh) for value. His check is more consistent with a hand like a straight or a set that is now looking to check-call or check-fold. I will bet for value, targeting those made straights and sets that may still feel compelled to call a reasonable bet in a heads-up pot. A bet of 2500 (50% of the pot) is a standard value-sizing here.

River call with pair of tens. Flash's bet of 2500 into a 7500 pot gives me 4:1 odds, requiring only ~20% equity to break even. My pair of tens (8cTc on Ad Kh Th 9c 5h) should have more than sufficient equity given Flash's calling pattern through two streets of aggression suggests a middling range rather than premium holdings. Flash's check-raise on the river after calling my flop and turn bets indicates either a draw that completed or a bluff trying to exploit my river check. Either way, the pot odds justify a call with a made hand that's likely ahead of bluffs and many weaker holdings.

JK is a premium holding in heads-up play. I'm on the button (small blind) facing Flash on the big blind. I have a significant positional advantage and should be aggressive with strong hands. At 50 chips to call, folding is not an option. A raise accomplishes several things: (1) takes the initiative preflop, (2) applies pressure on Flash's range, (3) allows me to win the pot immediately with fold equity, or (4) builds the pot when I have a strong hand. Raising to 250 (2.5x the big blind) is a standard aggressive raise size that applies meaningful pressure while keeping the pot manageable given my stack size of 15,025. This is a profitable long-term play in heads-up dynamics.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Seven of hearts. Claude has raised to 250 from the Button/Small Blind. Jack-Seven suited is a solid defending hand in a heads-up or blind-vs-blind situation, as it has good playability and flush/straight potential. I am getting good pot odds (calling 150 to win a total pot of 400). I will call to see a flop.

On the flop of King-Ace-Deuce, I have Jack-high with a back-door heart draw. This board strongly favors Claude's pre-flop raising range, which likely contains many Aces and Kings. I have very little equity here and no real reason to bluff into the pre-flop raiser on this specific texture. I will check and see what Claude decides to do, likely folding if he bets.

Top pair King-Jack on KAd2s board after Flash checked. Flash's check indicates likely weakness rather than premium hands (AA, AK). Standard continuation bet at 60% of pot applies pressure, builds pot when ahead, and extracts value from weaker holdings like KQ, KT, Broadway draws, or weak Aces. Positional advantage and preflop aggression support aggressive follow-up. Bet sizing of 300 keeps Flash engaged while maintaining control of the hand.

On a flop of Kc Ad 2s, the board heavily favors the pre-flop raiser, Claude. I have Jack-high with very little equity and only a remote backdoor flush draw. Claude's continuation bet indicates he is likely continuing with his range advantage. There is no strategic reason to call or bluff into this board texture with my current holding, as I am likely drawing thin or dead against his range of Aces and Kings.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with J8s, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. Claude is in the Big Blind. Standard strategy is to raise or complete from the button. Raising allows me to take control of the hand and potentially win the pot pre-flop. I'll make a standard 3x raise to 300.

9-3 offsuit is a very weak holding that doesn't warrant engagement, especially in a heads-up situation. While pot odds of 2:1 (400 to call 200) might mathematically suggest borderline equity, 9-3 is severely below average and will struggle significantly post-flop against any reasonable range from Flash. Flash has been playing aggressively throughout the session, but I should preserve my stack (15225) for stronger opportunities. We're at hand 71 of 150 with plenty of opportunities ahead. This is a clear fold scenario - no need to speculate with a marginal hand from the big blind against an aggressive opponent.

8♦2♠ is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. While I'm in position on the button against Flash's big blind, this hand lacks the strength to continue profitably. Even in heads-up play with wider ranges, 8-2 offsuit plays poorly post-flop with no strong equity. I need to only call with hands that have reasonable potential. This hand doesn't meet that threshold. Folding preserves chips for premium spots where I have a clear edge.
I am on the button with JTs, a very strong premium starting hand in heads-up play. Standard strategy is to raise to build the pot and take control of the hand. I will raise to 300.

3h 8h is one of the weakest hands in poker. Despite getting 3:1 pot odds, I'm significantly behind Flash's range and would be out of position post-flop. With a healthy 15075 chip stack and weak hand quality, this is an easy fold. I should preserve chips for better opportunities rather than chase marginal odds with such a weak holding.

Pocket Kings is one of the strongest starting hands in poker and should be played aggressively preflop. I'm in the Small Blind heads-up against Flash, who has 24,825 chips to my 15,025. I've already posted 50 as the SB, and Flash has posted 100 as BB. 

The pot is currently 150 with 50 to call. Raising to 300 total (a 3x raise over the BB) is standard aggression for a premium pair like Kings. This accomplishes several goals:
1. Builds the pot while my hand is strong
2. Applies pressure on Flash to make a decision
3. Gets value when Flash calls with weaker holdings
4. Keeps balanced aggression while in a heads-up dynamic

This is the textbook play for pocket Kings preflop in this situation.

I am in the big blind with 9d4h, a very weak hand. Claude has raised to 300 from the button/small blind. Even though the price to call is only 200 more chips to see a flop, this hand has poor playability and I am out of position. Given the stack depths and the fact that 9-4 offsuit is near the bottom of my range, folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack.

In a heads-up situation from the small blind/button, QJo (Queen-Jack offsuit) is a strong starting hand. It is well above the average hand and should be played aggressively to gain the initiative and extract value or force a fold from the big blind. I will raise to 3x the big blind, which is 300 chips.

Pocket fives in heads-up against a button raiser. With 200 to call in a 400 pot, I have favorable 2:1 odds. My pair has reasonable equity (~45-50%) against Flash's likely button range. Calling allows me to set-mine and play the hand based on the flop. Out-of-position, I prefer to keep the pot smaller with a marginal hand and let the board help me rather than 3-betting here.

Pocket fives on a K-Q-7 board is a marginal hand OOP. My pair is likely behind against Flash's button raising range. I should check to keep the pot small with a hand that needs improvement. This allows me to potentially see the turn card and set-mine with 2 remaining outs (two fives left in the deck). Betting would invite aggression from an in-position opponent where I'm likely behind. Checking also respects position and allows Flash to play his hand.

I have middle pair (Queens) with a Jack kicker on a board of Kd-Qs-7c. This is a solid made hand, but the King is a concern as it hits the range of the big blind (Claude) who called my pre-flop raise. Claude checked to me, which is standard. I should bet here to extract value from draws (like J-10 or 10-9) or smaller pairs (like pocket 8s, 9s, or a 7). A half-pot bet of 300 chips is appropriate to define where I am in the hand while maintaining control of the pot.

With pocket fives on a K-Q-7 board in heads-up play, I'm calling Flash's 300 flop bet. My hand analysis: I have a low pair with approximately 40-50% equity against Flash's likely range (which could include broadway cards, overpairs, and better pairs). The pot odds are favorable - I'm calling 300 into a 1200 chip pot (4:1), and I need only 25% equity to call profitably. Out of position with a marginal hand, calling is superior to folding (I'm often chopping equity with Flash's c-betting range) or raising (which likely gets called by better hands or forces Flash's bluffs out). Calling allows me to set-mine, keep the pot manageable, and see how the board develops on the turn where I might improve or get additional information about Flash's hand strength.

With pocket fives on a K-Q-7-A board, my hand has deteriorated significantly. The ace on the turn is a blank that likely improves Flash's range considerably (broadway cards, overpairs) while doing nothing for my low pair. I have approximately 2 outs to make a set, giving me roughly 4% equity on the river, which means my current pair has minimal equity against Flash's likely holdings. I am holding Ace-Trey offsuit in the Big Blind. My opponent, Claude, has limped from the Button. Ace-high is a strong hand in heads-up play and is likely ahead of a limping range. I will raise to 400 to take control of the hand and build a pot with my equity advantage.

Heads-up call with Js6s. Pot odds are 5:3 (need 37% equity), and I have solid equity against Flash's likely 3-bet range. Position post-flop is valuable, and suited gappers play well in position with deep stacks. Can hit straights, flushes, or top pair, making this a profitable call rather than folding or committing more with a marginal hand via 3-bet.

The flop of 7h-8h-4c is quite coordinated and likely favors Claude's limp-calling range, which often consists of middle connectors and small pocket pairs. Holding Ace-high with no direct draw, my hand is currently ahead of his air but behind any pair or significant draw. I will check to control the pot size and see how Claude responds to this wet board texture.

Betting 300 on the flop with Js6s against Flash's check. I have a gutshot straight draw (8 outs) plus overcards and backdoor flush draw, giving me substantial equity. Flash's check suggests potential weakness or a draw. A 300 bet (~3/8 pot) applies pressure, wins pots immediately vs weak hands, and gets value from medium-strength hands. With position and drawing potential, aggressive play maximizes my edge here.

Claude's limp-call pre-flop suggests a range that includes many middle-coordinated hands and small pairs, which connect well with this 7h-8h-4c board. However, his bet of 300 into a pot of 800 is a relatively small sizing (37.5% pot). My Ace-high (with the Ace of hearts) still has reasonable equity against his semi-bluffs and drawing range, and I have two overcards that could give me the best hand on the turn. Given the pot odds, I will call to see a turn card and re-evaluate based on the next street's action and board development.

The turn brings the Queen of hearts, a card that could potentially improve Claude's range if he was floating with hands like Q9, QT, or QJ. I currently hold Ace-high with the Ace of hearts, which gives me the nut flush draw blocker. Since I am out of position and have not improved to a pair, checking is the most strategic play to control the pot and evaluate Claude's interest in the hand on this street.

Betting 600 on the turn with Js6s is optimal. I have a gutshot straight draw (8 outs) plus overcard potential, giving me solid equity. Flash's check indicates weakness after calling my flop bet. This semi-bluff bet applies pressure, wins pots immediately vs weak holdings, and maintains strong equity if called. With position and reasonable drawing odds, this is a profitable aggressive play against Flash's apparent weakness.

On the turn, the Queen of hearts completes the flush draw I was blocking with the Ace of hearts, but it also gives me the Nut Flush Draw. Claude has bet 600 into a 1400 pot (roughly 43% pot). With the Nut Flush Draw and Ace-high, I have significant equity to continue. My 9 outs to the flush give me approximately an 18-20% chance to improve on the river, and the pot odds (600 to call for a total pot of 2600) require about 23% equity. Given that my Ace might also be an out and I have the nut blocker to his potential flushes, calling is the most appropriate action to realize my equity and potentially take the pot down on the river if I hit or if Claude is bluffing.

On the river, the Ace of Diamonds gives me top pair. However, the heart flush was completed on the turn, and Claude has shown aggression by betting both the flop and the turn. I hold the Ace of hearts, which is a crucial blocker to the nut flush. Since I have been in check-call mode throughout the hand, checking the river is the most consistent and strategic play. It allows me to induce a bluff from Claude's missed straight draws (like 56 or 9T) or potentially get to a cheap showdown if he checks behind with a hand like a Queen. If he bets, I will have to evaluate the sizing, but my top pair and nut flush blocker make it a strong candidate for a call.

Checking back on the river with Jack-high. My semi-bluff run (gutshot with overcards on flop/turn) has completed without improvement. Flash's passive line (checking and calling) suggests a moderate-strength holding that I likely can't out-bluff. With only Jack-high and insufficient fold equity against a calling opponent, checking preserves chips and allows showdown. Bluffing the river would be exploitable given Flash's demonstrated calling tendency.

KTo is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, well within the top 20-25% of hands. Raising from the button/small blind is the standard aggressive play to take control of the pot and apply pressure on the big blind. I will raise to 300 chips.

Calling the 200 additional chips (for a total of 300 to the pot). While 6-Jack is weak and I'm out of position, the 2:1 pot odds and already-committed 100 BB make this a borderline call. Flash's wide raising range means my equity is close to break-even. I can evaluate the flop and make decisions post-flop based on what develops.

Checking the flop is prudent here. The board is three diamonds (5d-7d-6d), which is dangerous when I have no diamonds. My pair of sixes is decent but not strong enough to bet for value. Flash's check after preflop raising suggests either weakness or caution about the diamond-heavy board. By checking back, I keep the pot manageable, maintain pot control, and gather information on the turn card. My hand is in reasonable shape to evaluate further street-by-street.

On this highly coordinated 5-6-7 diamond board, I have the King of Diamonds, giving me the second-best possible flush draw. This is an excellent hand to continuation bet. By betting, I can fold out some of Claude's air that doesn't have a diamond, and if I am called, I have significant equity to improve to a flush or potentially top pair. I will bet 300, which is half the pot, to maintain aggression.

Calling 300 to reach 1200 in the pot. I'm getting 4:1 pot odds and need only 25% equity. My pair of sixes likely has 35-40% equity against Flash's range of draws and strong hands. While the three-diamond board is dangerous, staying in position to reassess the turn is prudent. If another diamond hits, I can fold to future aggression, but a blank allows me to continue with my reasonable holding.

Checking the turn to control the pot. My pair of sixes is vulnerable on this three-diamond board. Flash's check suggests caution, and betting my medium-strength hand would bloat the pot when I'm likely behind to his range. Checking keeps the decision simple and allows me to evaluate the river card and Flash's river action before committing more chips.

The turn Queen of Clubs doesn't improve my hand, but I still hold the King of Diamonds, which gives me the second-best flush draw on this monochrome board. The board is also highly coordinated for straights (5-6-7). Given that Claude called the flop bet, it's likely he has a made hand or a significant draw. By checking behind, I take a free card to try and hit my flush or a King on the river while controlling the pot size on a dangerous board.

On the river with a pair of sixes (6h-6d with Q-8-7-5 kickers), I should check this down. Flash has checked both the turn and river, indicating weakness—likely a missed flush draw or a weaker holding than my pair. Given that Flash initially raised preflop and bet the flop aggressively, his two consecutive checks suggest his hand missed key draws or he's controlling the pot with a marginal hand.

Betting the river accomplishes little: (1) stronger hands call and win, (2) weaker hands fold, giving me no additional value, (3) I risk getting checkraised. My pair of sixes isn't strong enough to extract value against Flash's likely missed hands, but it's also vulnerable to showdown pressure if I bet.

Checking allows me to reach showdown with my pair and either win the pot as-is or lose the minimum if Flash has a better hand. This is the most profitable play given the action and my hand strength.

On the river, the board is 5d-7d-6d-Qc-8c. My hand is King-high with the King of Diamonds. While I have the second-best flush blocker, the board is extremely coordinated with three diamonds and a four-to-a-straight (5-6-7-8). Claude called my flop bet, which suggests he has a piece of this board, either a pair, a diamond, or a straight draw. After he checked the turn and I checked behind, his check on the river is consistent with a marginal hand or a missed draw. While I could attempt a bluff, the likelihood of him folding any pair or a straight is low on this board. King-high has very little showdown value, but checking back is the most prudent play to avoid losing more chips in a spot where a bluff is unlikely to be successful against a range that called the flop.

Heads-up button raise with A4o. Standard aggressive play in position - either takes down the pot immediately or allows me to play the flop in position. A4o is above the bottom of my raising range in this spot. This raise size (2.5x BB) maintains consistent aggression shown throughout the session.

In a heads-up situation, 8c 5c is a standard defending hand from the Big Blind against a 2.5x raise. The pot odds (150 to call into a total pot of 350) are favorable, and the hand has decent post-flop playability due to being suited. Given our significant chip lead over Claude, calling here to see a flop is the most appropriate play.

I have completely missed this flop with 8-high and no real draws. The board of A-Q-9 is very favorable for the pre-flop raiser's range (Claude), as it contains two high cards and a spade draw. There is no reason to lead out here. I will check and plan to fold to most bets, as my hand has almost no equity to continue.

I have top pair (Ace) with a 4 kicker on a 9♠Q♣A♠ flop after raising pre-flop with A4o from the button. Flash checked, indicating weakness or a marginal hand. This is an excellent spot to value bet because: (1) I likely have the best hand against Flash's checking range, (2) I want to build the pot with top pair, (3) I need to protect against draws (particularly flush draws with two spades on board), and (4) my aggressive style throughout this session warrants continuation betting in position after my raise. A bet of 350 (approximately 2/3 pot) is consistent with my established aggression while getting value from weaker holdings like Q, 9, or lower pairs that Flash might call with.

The flop of As Qc 9s is very unfavorable for my 8c 5c, as it connects heavily with the pre-flop raiser's range. I have no pair and no real draws. Facing a 70% pot-sized bet from Claude, there is no justification for continuing with eight-high. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

In a heads-up match, the button should be opening a very wide range of hands. Although 2d 3d is a weak hand, its suitedness gives it some post-flop playability. I will raise to 300 to maintain my aggressive pressure from the button and try to take down the pot pre-flop. If called, I will have the advantage of position throughout the rest of the hand.

K5o is too weak to profitably defend the big blind against an aggressive button raiser. While pot odds are reasonable at 2.25:1, the hand lacks showdown strength and will be difficult to play out of position post-flop. Folding preserves chips for premium situations.

Heads-up button raise with A2o. Position and initiative are paramount in 2-player dynamics. A2o is in my raising range from the button. This raise either wins the blinds or allows me to play in position post-flop with a hand that can flop top pair or Ace-high. Standard 3x sizing.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Trey offsuit. Claude has raised to 200 from the Small Blind. Although I am getting 3:1 odds to call, this hand is extremely weak and does not play well post-flop, especially out of position. I prefer to fold and wait for a more favorable hand to compete with.

I am on the button with 7c 4s, which is a very weak starting hand even in heads-up play. Given my significant stack lead and the fact that Claude has been defending aggressively and playing well post-flop, there is no need to play trash hands out of position or inflate a pot with a sub-optimal holding. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Folding 2-9 offsuit on the button. Despite positional advantage, this is one of the worst hands in poker with minimal equity. With a moderately short stack, preserving chips for premium hands is more valuable than attempting to steal with trash cards against an aggressive opponent.

I am on the button with KQo, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and maintain initiative, as this hand performs well against the range of hands the Big Blind (Claude) will typically defend with.

Folding 3-2o (worst starting hand) from the big blind. Despite having 2:1 pot odds, this hand plays terribly post-flop, particularly out of position against an aggressive opponent. No realistic way to win without hitting specific cards, and even then I'd be in marginal spots. Preserving chips for stronger opportunities is the correct long-term strategy.

Folding 2-Js (deuce-jack offsuit) from the button in heads-up play. While heads-up ranges are wider and pot odds are 3:1, this hand is too weak and lacks playability. With a shorter stack against Flash's superior chip position, I should preserve capital and wait for stronger holdings where I have better equity. Flash's aggressive tendencies mean I need quality hands to profitably engage.
I have Ace-King suited, which is a premier starting hand in heads-up play. I will raise to 300 to build the pot for value and maintain aggression from the button.

Folding Q-3 offsuit in the Big Blind facing a button raise from Flash. This hand lacks strong kicker strength and I'm out of position postflop. With 50 hands remaining and a reasonable stack size (12525), I should be selective and wait for better opportunities. The hand is too weak to defend profitably even with moderate pot odds when considering positional disadvantage.

Folding J5o from the small blind. This is a marginal hand with poor equity that doesn't warrant engagement, especially given the 2:1 chip deficit against Flash. Need to be selective and wait for premium holdings to regain chip lead.

I hold KQo on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a premium starting hand in this context. My standard opening raise has been to 300 throughout the match, and I will maintain that sizing to put pressure on the big blind and build a pot with a superior range.

4-6 offsuit is a very weak hand with minimal winning equity. While Flash's button raise could be a wide range, I'm facing a 200-chip call with marginal hand strength and only two players. Given that I'm relatively short-stacked (12,375 chips, ~120 BB), I should preserve capital for stronger opportunities. 4-6o plays poorly post-flop even if I hit a piece, and folding is the mathematically correct play. This maintains good aggression on better hands while avoiding marginal spots.

Playing 4h5h on the button in heads-up against Flash. This is hand #104 of 150. In heads-up play, position is critical and I should be raising a substantial portion of my button holdings to apply pressure and leverage positional advantage. 4-5 suited has reasonable playability post-flop and can make straights and flushes. By raising to 200 (min raise), I'm applying aggression without overcommitting with this marginal hand. Flash will need to defend with a wide range from the big blind, and if he folds, I win the pot immediately. If he calls, I maintain position post-flop with a hand that plays reasonably in position. Given my stack size (12,325) vs. Flash's (27,525), I need to accumulate chips by playing aggressively from position.

In this heads-up situation, I'm in the Big Blind with Q2o and Claude has raised to 200 from the Button. While Q2o is a weak hand, I'm getting 3:1 odds to call (100 to win a total pot of 300). In a heads-up game with deep stacks, defending the big blind with a wide range is standard to avoid being exploited by frequent button raises. I will call to see a flop and hope to connect or find an opportunity to outplay my opponent post-flop.

On a flop of K94 rainbow, my Q-high has very little equity. Claude raised pre-flop and this board texture generally favors his range more than mine. I have no pair, no draw, and only one overcard to the nine and four. Since the action is checked to me (or I am first to act), I will check. If he bets, I will most likely fold as there's no reason to invest more chips with such a weak hand.

Heads-up position play: Flash checked the 9-4-K flop, indicating weakness. I have a pair of fours with multiple straight draw possibilities (gutshots). A continuation bet of 300 (75% of pot) applies pressure on Flash's weak check while maintaining manageable pot size given my shorter stack. In heads-up play, position control and aggression are critical - this bet either wins immediately or gets valuable information about Flash's holding. My marginal hand is best played aggressively here rather than passively checking behind.

On a flop of K-9-4 rainbow, I have nothing but Queen-high with a Deuce kicker. Claude's pre-flop raise and continuation bet of 300 into a 400 pot indicate strength or a standard C-bet on a board that favors his opening range (which includes many Kings and better pairs). I have no draw and virtually no equity to continue. Folding is the only sensible move here to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 8h 4d, which is a very weak hand. Even in a heads-up situation against Claude, 8-high offsuit doesn't play well post-flop and doesn't have enough equity to justify a raise or even a call to see a flop. Claude has shown aggression in previous hands, and I want to wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead rather than bleeding chips with marginal or sub-marginal holdings.

Heads-up on the button with 6dTd - a suited connector with solid playability. In heads-up poker, positional advantage is critical and standard strategy dictates opening a wide range from the button. 6dTd has straight and flush potential. Raising to 200 (doubling the BB) is the standard aggressive button play that exploits position, maintains initiative, and follows profitable heads-up strategy.

I am holding Ace-Eight offsuit in the Big Blind in a heads-up situation. This is a strong hand against a standard button opening range. Claude raised to 200 (a min-raise). I will 3-bet to 700 to take the initiative and build a pot with a hand that likely has superior equity to his range.

Folding 6dTd to Flash's 3-bet. While the pot odds are reasonable, being out of position post-flop with a marginal hand against an aggressive opponent is not profitable. The hand lacks the strength to justify continued investment, and I have the stack flexibility to wait for better spots with premium holdings or stronger playable hands. Discretion is the better part of valor in mid-tier hand situations.

Holding Ace-Deuce offsuit on the button in a heads-up situation, I have a range advantage. Ace-high is a strong starting hand in this context. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and put pressure on Claude in the big blind.

Flash has raised to 300 from the button/small blind. I'm in the big blind with A3o, which is a marginal hand but playable in this spot. Key considerations: (1) I have position post-flop, (2) Flash has been aggressively raising from the button on recent hands (95, 99, 101, 103) suggesting a wide opening range, (3) I'm getting 2:1 pot odds (200 to call into 400 pot), (4) A3o has decent equity against a wide button-raising range, (5) Stack sizes are deep enough for post-flop play (I have ~82 BBs). While A3o is weak in absolute terms, defending the big blind against button aggression is standard strategy. I can play reasonably post-flop with an ace and position. Calling is more appropriate than folding or raising given the hand strength and board dynamics.

Checking is the only reasonable action with A3o on a paired board (Th Td 7c). My hand has minimal equity and cannot stand pressure. A bet would only lose chips against Flash's likely holdings, while checking allows me to see the turn card cheaply and control the pot size. The board is relatively dry, so checking also keeps Flash's aggression in check while I wait for clarifying information from his turn action.

I raised pre-flop with Ace-high and was called by Claude. The flop is 7c-Th-Td, a paired board which often misses both ranges, but I hold an Ace as a potential overcard and for showdown value. Claude checked. This board is generally good for the pre-flop aggressor to maintain pressure with a continuation bet, as it's hard for him to have a Ten frequently, and I can represent high pairs or better. I will bet 300 to take the pot down now or set up a barrel on later streets.

A3o cannot justify a call on this double-paired board (Th Td 7c). The hand is dominated and has minimal equity (~20% at best) against Flash's preflop raising range, which includes many strong holdings (sets, overpairs, premium aces). While I have positional advantage as the big blind, my hand weakness is too severe. Even with 4:1 pot odds, my actual equity doesn't support a call. Folding is the correct decision.

Playing heads-up on the button with A-7o. This is a clear raising hand in heads-up play, especially from the small blind where I have positional advantage. An ace provides strong equity against Flash's big blind range, and the seven provides additional value. A min-raise to 200 is appropriate here - it's aggressive enough to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable should I need to continue post-flop. Given that Flash has been somewhat aggressive but not overly so, this raise size gives me good implied odds if Flash calls and I flop well. My current chip position (12,075) allows me to play reasonably aggressive poker without risking too much. This is a standard profitable raise from this position.

I have T8o in the Big Blind. Claude has min-raised to 200. With 3:1 pot odds and the hand strength relative to a wide heads-up opening range, T8o is a standard defend. I will call the additional 100 chips to see the flop.

I have Ten-high on a King-high dry board. I missed the flop completely and have no draws. As the Big Blind, I will check to the pre-flop aggressor and evaluate their action. There is no reason to lead into the King here.

Flash checked the flop after calling my preflop raise, indicating a weak range. The board (3h-4c-Kd) is relatively dry and doesn't connect well with most BB calling ranges. I'm on the button with positional advantage and A7 is a reasonable hand that plays well in heads-up situations.

With Flash checking, this is a clear spot for a continuation bet. My ace gives me overcards and potential equity. A bet of around 250 chips (60% pot bet) accomplishes several goals:
1. Wins the pot immediately if Flash is weak (which the check indicates)
2. Builds the pot when I have decent equity
3. Maintains aggression in heads-up play where position is crucial
4. Keeps my aggressive image against Flash's relatively passive checking pattern

This is a standard c-bet in position against a weak-looking check. Flash's stack is much deeper than mine, so I need to be careful not to get into protracted betting wars, but a single bet here is appropriate sizing.

I have Ten-high on a King-high dry board with no draws and no pair. Claude's continuation bet on a King-high texture is very standard and likely to represent a wide range, but with zero equity and no backdoor potential, I cannot justify a call or a float here. Folding is the standard play.

I am on the button with Q3 offsuit. In heads-up play, Q-high is a reasonable hand to enter the pot with against the big blind, especially given my large stack advantage. However, Q3o is near the bottom of a standard raising range. Claude has been relatively active but hasn't shown extreme aggression. A standard 3x raise to 300 is a good way to apply pressure and potentially take the pot down pre-flop, or at least maintain initiative if called. This keeps my play consistent with my previous raises on the button.

Folding A2d from the big blind against Flash's button raise. This hand is too weak to defend out of position. While A2 has some value in heads-up play, the combination of (1) poor hand quality, (2) out-of-position disadvantage, (3) Flash's demonstrated aggression on future streets, and (4) lack of fold equity makes this a clear fold. Preserving my stack for premium hands where I have better position or equity is the optimal long-term strategy.

K9 suited from the button in heads-up is a premium hand that warrants aggressive play. I'm raising to 250 (2.5x big blind) to build the pot while utilizing my positional advantage. This is a standard aggressive button raise that applies pressure while allowing me to continue with good equity on most flop textures. The hand plays well post-flop from position.

I am in the Big Blind with K8o. Claude has raised to 250 from the Button. I have a stack of over 27,000, and he has about 12,000. The pot odds are favorable (150 to call to win 500 total), making this a standard defense against a wide Button opening range. I will call to see the flop.

On a monotone flop of As 2s 3s, I have the 8s, giving me a mediocre flush draw. As the Big Blind, I should check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how he reacts to this board, which heavily favors a range that contains many spades or an Ace.

K9 of hearts is too weak on this three-spade board (A♠2♠3♠). I have K-high with no draws and am vulnerable to Aces, pairs, and spade draws in Flash's range. Flash's check suggests checking is acceptable. By checking, I control pot size, avoid putting chips in with a weak hand, and give myself a chance to improve on the turn (catching a K or 9). Betting doesn't accomplish much - I'm unlikely to have dominated holdings to force out, and I'll face significant resistance from Flash's actual holdings or draws.

On the turn Qd following a checked flop of As 2s 3s, I still hold the 8s for a mid-flush draw. Claude's check on the flop indicates he likely doesn't have a made flush or a very strong Ace, or he is playing a marginal hand cautiously. By checking again, I allow Claude to lead with his marginal hands or continue to check down, giving me a free card to potentially hit my flush or King on the river without bloating the pot with a mediocre draw.

Flash has checked twice (flop and turn), indicating a weak hand or missed draw. My K♥9♥ is too weak to bet on this dangerous three-spade board with an Ace. By checking, I control the pot size, avoid putting money in with a weak hand, and get a free card on the river. If a spade comes, I'm beat anyway; if K or 9 hits, I improve. Betting accomplishes nothing except building the pot with a hand that's likely losing. Checking is the disciplined choice here.

On a board of As 2s 3s Qd 8d, I have a pair of 8s with a King kicker. The action was checked on both the flop and the turn, which strongly suggests that Claude does not have a strong hand like a flush, an Ace, or a Queen. By checking the river, I am looking to reach showdown for free or induce a bluff from a missed draw or weaker high cards. If Claude checks behind, my pair of 8s is likely the best hand in a pot that has remained small.

This is a straightforward check decision. I have K♥9♥ which is just K-high on a dangerous board (A♠2♠3♠Q♦8♦). Flash has checked all three streets (flop, turn, and river), indicating a weak hand or missed draw. While I could attempt a bluff here, the risk/reward is unfavorable: Flash's repeated checks suggest weakness, but the board is so scary (three spades, Ace-high, pairs possible) that he likely has something like a pocket pair or weak Ace that calls my bet. My hand has some showdown value against whiffed draws and weak holdings, so going to showdown with K-high is the disciplined play. Checking also keeps the pot small with a marginal hand, which is appropriate in this situation. This is a low-variance, correct line against an opponent who has demonstrated weakness.

I am on the button with 6d 4d, a suited one-gapper. In a heads-up situation, this is a reasonable hand to open or call with, especially with a deep stack of 27975 compared to Claude's 11875. Claude is in the Big Blind. Raising here is the standard aggressive play to take control of the pot and put pressure on the opponent. I will raise to 300.

I'm holding Ace-Ten offsuit in the Big Blind facing Flash's raise to 300. This is a solid hand in heads-up play. With a healthy stack of 11,875 vs Flash's 27,725, I should take an aggressive approach here.

Ace-Ten is strong enough to reraise preflop - it's in the top tier of hands I'd normally play from the Big Blind. By reraising to 800 (roughly 2.7x Flash's raise), I:
1. Take the initiative and apply pressure
2. Gain information about Flash's hand strength
3. Create fold equity against marginal hands
4. Build the pot when I have a decent hand
5. Maintain aggression in heads-up play

This is a standard aggressive play that balances my range while building a pot with a strong hand. The 800 raise is aggressive but not so large that it commits too much of my stack, leaving me plenty of room to play postflop decisions.

I opened to 300 from the button with 6d 4d, and Claude 3-bet to 800. The price to call is 500 more into a pot of 1100, meaning I need approximately 31% equity to justify a call based on pot odds alone. Given that I have a suited one-gapper with good playability and I will be in position for the remainder of the hand, this is a standard call against a 3-bet in a heads-up match. I also have significant implied odds if I hit a strong hand against Claude's 11k stack.

Continuation betting on the flop after my preflop 3-bet. With A♠T♣ on 7♦2♠6♥, I have two high overcards plus a gutshot draw, giving me reasonable equity. The dry flop aligns with my preflop aggression. A bet of 800 (50% of pot) maintains pressure, represents strength, and gains information about Flash's holding. This is a standard aggressive play in heads-up poker that keeps the initiative.

On a flop of 7d-2s-6h, I have middle pair (sixes) with a backdoor flush draw. Claude 3-bet pre-flop and has now led out for 800 into a 1600 pot. This is a standard continuation bet from a 3-bettor. My hand is strong enough to call one street, especially in position, to see how the turn develops. I am currently ahead of his high-card hands like AK or AQ, while trailing overpairs like 88-JJ. Folding would be too conservative given the pot odds and the strength of a pair in a heads-up match.

Checking the turn with A♠T♣ on 7♦2♠6♥6♠. The turn 6♠ doesn't improve my hand and potentially helps Flash's range (possible 6-x holdings). Top pair with a weak kicker is vulnerable on a paired board. By checking, I control the pot, maintain a showdown opportunity with my top pair, and avoid over-committing when Flash shows willingness to call. This gives me more information on the river to make a better decision.

On the turn, the board has paired with a six, giving me trip sixes (6d 6h 6s). Claude, who was the pre-flop 3-bettor and the flop lead, has now checked. This check often indicates either a trap with a very strong hand (like an overpair or 77) or, more likely, a realization that his high-card hands (like AK or AQ) have lost equity. Since I have a very strong hand, I want to bet for value to get called by his overpairs (88 through AA) or any seven he might be holding. A bet of 1600 (half the pot) is a standard value size that keeps his calling range wide while building the pot for a potential river commitment.

Analyzing this turn decision carefully: Flash raised from 300 preflop, I 3-bet to 800, he called. I c-bet the flop for 800 on 7♦2♠6♥, Flash called. The turn brought 6♠, and I checked what I thought was 'top pair' but actually I have A♠T♣—just ace-high with an overcard ten on a 7♦2♠6♥6♠ board. This is a critical reassessment. 

Flash's turn raise to 1,600 into the 4,800 pot suggests the 6♠ helped him significantly—likely made trips (6x), two pair (7x), or he's semi-bluffing with draws. My previous analysis incorrectly identified my hand as 'top pair with weak kicker' when I actually have no made hand at all.

Equity calculation: A♠T♣ needs roughly 25% equity to break even (1,600/6,400), but against Flash's likely range after this aggression (strong pairs, two pair, trips), my ace-high probably holds only 20-25% equity at best—marginally breakeven at the math but worse when considering:

1. I showed weakness with my turn check, inviting aggression
2. Flash's raise indicates clear strength on a board that improved his likely holdings
3. My hand is extremely vulnerable with four cards still unknown
4. In heads-up play, exploitability matters—calling with marginal equity and weak hands that showed weakness is exploitable
5. I have 8,775 chips remaining and maintain a decent stack to play better spots

Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities where I have clearer equity or where I haven't signaled weakness.

In heads-up play from the button/small blind, A3o is within a reasonable raising range. While it's not a premium hand, the ace plays well in a 2-player game. Raising to 250 (small raise) applies pressure, takes the initiative, and balances my range. Given my short stack situation, I need to avoid being too passive while still respecting pot odds. This raise sets up the action and puts Flash to a decision.

In a heads-up situation, Ten-Nine offsuit is a strong enough hand to defend the Big Blind against a standard 2.5x raise. My opponent, Claude, has a stack of about 100 big blinds, and I have a significant chip lead overall. T9o has good playability and connectivity. I will call the additional 150 to see a flop and evaluate based on the board texture.

I have middle pair (Nines) with a Ten kicker on a board of K-9-6. As the Big Blind checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard play here. My hand is strong enough to check-call a bet from Claude, but not strong enough to lead out or check-raise for value. I will check to see Claude's reaction and evaluate the turn based on the bet sizing.

Continuation bet on the flop after Flash checks. My A3o has decent equity, and Flash's check suggests weakness or a marginal hand. Betting 325 (65% of pot) applies pressure, maintains my aggressive image in heads-up play, and wins the pot outright if Flash is indeed weak. This sizing is appropriate given my stack size and the need to avoid being exploited as too passive.

I have middle pair (Nines) with a decent kicker (Ten) on a board of King-Nine-Six. My opponent, Claude, is the pre-flop aggressor and is making a standard continuation bet. Middle pair is a strong enough hand to continue with in a heads-up pot, especially since I'm in position. Raising here would likely only be called by a King or a better Nine, while folding is too weak. I will call to see the turn and evaluate the action based on the card and Claude's subsequent betting behavior.
